entry.title: Curvature and Differential Forms
entry.content:
2Curvature and Differential Forms

第一章我们用向量场的语言发展了\(\mathbb{R}^3\)中曲线和曲面的曲率的经典理论。有一个对偶的使用微分形式的方法。微分形式即使我们只关心向量场也会自然出现。比如，开集上一个坐标系中切向量的系数是开集上的微分1形式。微分形式比向量场更微妙：它们可以被微分和相乘，并且它们在光滑映射的拉回下有意义。在1920s和1930s，Élie Cartan引领了微分形式在微分几何中的应用，并且这些被证明是强大通用的工具。

这一章中我们用微分形式重新发展联络和曲率的理论。首先，第10节把联络从一个切丛扩展到任意向量丛。11节我们展示如何用微分形式表示联络、曲率和挠率。最后，为了展示它们的用处，我们在12节用微分形式重新证明高斯绝妙定理。

10Connections on a Vector Bundle

仿射联络将方向导数从 \(\mathbb{R}^n\) 推广到一个任意流形，但是不包括沿着 \(\mathbb{R}^n\) 中一个子流形的向量场的方向导数的情形。为此，我们需要向量丛上联络的记号。一个流形上的仿射联络不过是切丛上的联络。因为向量丛上的联络 \( abla_X s\) 中两个变量的不对称性，挠率不再有定义，但是曲率仍然有意义。

我们在向量丛上定义黎曼度量，使得流形上的黎曼度量变成切丛上的黎曼度量。联络和度量的相容性 compactibility 仍然有意义。但是，缺少挠率的概念让我们不能像黎曼流形上的黎曼联络那样再找出一个向量丛上唯一的联络。

向量丛上的一个联络显然是一个局部算子，和其他所有局部算子一样，它可以被限制在任意开子集上。

10.1Connections on a Vector Bundle

定义 10.1. Let \(E ightarrow M\) be a \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundle over \(M\). A connection on \(E\) is a map

\(\displaystyle abla : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (E)\)

such that for \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(s \in \Gamma (E)\),

  1. \( abla_X s\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(X\) and \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear in \(s\);

  2. (Leibniz rule) if \(f\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) function on \(M\), then

    \(\displaystyle abla_X (f s) = (X f) s + f abla_X s.\)

Since \(X f = (d f) X\), the Leibniz rule may be rewritten as

\(\displaystyle abla_X (f s) = (d f) (X) s + f abla_X s,\)

or suppressing \(X\),

\(\displaystyle abla (f s) = d f \cdot s + f abla s.\)

例. An affine connection on a manifold \(M\) is a connection on the tangent bundle \(T M ightarrow M\).

例 10.2. Let \(M\) be a submanifold of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and \(E = T\mathbb{R}^n |_M\) the restriction of the tangent bundle of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) on \(M\). In Section 4.5, we defined the directional derivative

\(\displaystyle D : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M) ightarrow \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M) .\)

By Proposition 4.9, it is a connection on the vector bundle \(T\mathbb{R}^n |_M\).

We say that a section \(s \in \Gamma (E)\) is flat if \( abla_X s = 0\) for all \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\).

例 10.3. (Induced connection on a trivial bundle). Let \(E\) be a trivial bundle of rank \(r\) over a manifold \(M\). Thus, there is a bundle isomorphism \(\phi : E ightarrow M imes \mathbb{R}^r\), called a trivialization for \(E\) over \(M\). The trivialization \(\phi : E \xrightarrow{\sim} M imes \mathbb{R}^r\) induces a connection on \(E\) as follows. If \(v_1, \ldots, v_r\) is a basis for \(\mathbb{R}^r\), then \(s_i : p \mapsto (p, v_i), i = 1, \ldots, r\), define a global frame for the product bundle \(M imes \mathbb{R}^r\) over \(M\), and \(e_i = \phi^{- 1} \circ s_i, i = 1, \ldots, r\), define a global frame for \(E\) over \(M\):

So every section \(s \in \Gamma (E)\) can be written uniquely as a linear combination

\(\displaystyle s = \sum h^i e_i, \quad h^i \in \mathcal{F}.\)

We can define a connection \( abla\) on \(E\) by declaring the section \(e_i\) to be flat and applying the Leibniz rule and \(\mathbb{R}\)-linearity to define \( abla_X s\):

\(\displaystyle abla_X s = abla_X \left( \sum h^i e_i ight) = \sum (X h^i) e_i .\) (10.1)

例 10.4. Check that (10.1) defines a connection on the trivial bundle \(E\).

The connection \( abla\) on a trivial bundle induced by a trivialization depends on the trivialization, for the flat sections for \( abla\) are precisely the sections of \(E\) corresponding to the constant sections of \(M imes \mathbb{R}^r\) under the trivialization.

10.2Existence of a Connection on a Vector Bundle

10.1 节我们定义了向量丛上的联络，并展示了一个平凡丛上的联络。我们来证明一个任意向量丛上的联络的存在性。

Let \( abla^0, abla^1\) be two connections on a vector bundle \(E\) over \(M\). By the Leibniz rule, for any vector field \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), section \(s \in \Gamma (E)\), and function \(f \in C^{\infty} (M)\),

\(\displaystyle abla_X^0 (f s) = (X f) s + f abla_X^0 s,\) (10.2)
\(\displaystyle abla_X^1 (f s) = (X f) s + f abla_X^1 s.\) (10.3)

Hence,

\(\displaystyle ( abla_X^0 + abla_X^1) (f s) = 2 (X f) s + f ( abla_X^0 + abla_X^1) s.\) (10.4)

Because of the extra factor 2 in (10.4) the sum of two connections does not satisfy the Leibniz rule and so is not a connection. However, if we multiply (10.2) by \(1 - t\) and (10.3) by \(t\), then \((1 - t) abla_X^0 + t abla_X^1\) satisfies the Leibniz rule.

命题 10.5. Any finite linear combination \(\sum t_i abla^i\) of connections \( abla^i\) is a connection provided the coefficients add up to 1, \(\sum t_i = 1\).

A finite linear combination whose coefficients add up to 1 is called a convex linear combination.

定理 10.6. Every \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundle \(E\) over a manifold \(M\) has a connection.

10.3Curvature of a Connection on a Vector Bundle

挠率的概念对任意一个向量丛上的联络不再有意义，但是曲率仍然有。它由仿射联络的相同的公式定义: for \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(s \in \Gamma (E)\),

\(\displaystyle R (X, Y) s = abla_X abla_Y s - abla_Y abla_X s - abla_{[X, Y]} s \in \Gamma (E) .\)

So \(R\) is an \(\mathbb{R}\)-multilinear map

\(\displaystyle \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (E) .\)

As before, \(R (X, Y)\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in all three arguments and so it is actually defined pointwise. Moreover, because \(R_p (X_p, Y_p)\) is skew-symmetric in \(X_p\) and \(Y_p\), at every point \(p\) there is an alternating bilinear map

\(\displaystyle R_p : T_p M imes T_p M ightarrow \operatorname{Hom} (E_p, E_p) =: \operatorname{End} (E_p)\)

into the endomorphism ring of \(E_p\). We call this map the curvature tensor of the connection.

10.4Riemannian Bundles

We can also generalize the notion of a Riemannian metric to vector bundles. Let \(E ightarrow M\) be a \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundle over a manifold \(M\). A Riemannian metric on \(E\) assigns to each \(p \in M\) an inner product \(\langle, angle_p\) on the fiber \(E_p\); the assignment is required to be \(C^{\infty}\) in the following sense: if \(s\) and \(t\) are \(C^{\infty}\) sections of \(E\), then \(\langle s, t angle\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) function on \(M\).

Thus, a Riemannian metric on a manifold \(M\) is simply a Riemannian metric on the tangent bundle \(T M\). A vector bundle together with a Riemannian metric is called a Riemannian bundle.

例 10.7. Let \(E\) be a trivial vector bundle of rank \(r\) over a maniold \(M\), with trivialization \(\phi : E \xrightarrow{\sim} M imes \mathbb{R}^r\). The Euclidean inner product \(\langle, angle_{\mathbb{R}^r}\) on \(\mathbb{R}^r\) induces a Riemannian metric on \(E\) via the trivialization \(\phi\): if \(u, v \in E_p\), then the fiber map \(\phi_p : E_p ightarrow \mathbb{R}^r\) is a linear isomorphism and we define

\(\displaystyle \langle u, v angle = \langle \phi_p (u), \phi_p (v) angle_{\mathbb{R}^r} .\)

It is easy to check that \(\langle, angle\) is a Riemannian metric on \(E\).

The proof of Theorem 1.12 generalizes to prove the existence of a Riemannian metric on a vector bundle.

定理 10.8. On any \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundle \(\pi : E ightarrow M\), there is a Riemannian metric.

10.5Metric Connections

We say that a connection \( abla\) on a Riemannian bundle \(E\) is compatible with the metric if for all \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(s, t \in \Gamma (E)\),

\(\displaystyle X \langle s, t angle = \langle abla_X s, t angle + \langle s, abla_X t angle .\)

A connection compatible with the metric on a Riemannian bundle is also called a metric connection.

例. Let \(E\) be a trivial vector bundle of rank \(r\) over a manifold \(M\), with trivialization \(\phi : E \xrightarrow{\sim} M imes \mathbb{R}^r\). We showed in Example 10.3 that the trivializaiton induces a connection \( abla\) on \(E\) and in Example 10.7 that the trivialization induces a Riemannian metric on \(E\).

命题 10.9. On a trivial vector bundle \(E\) over a manifold \(M\) with trivialization \(\phi : E \xrightarrow{\sim} M imes \mathbb{R}^r\), the connection \( abla\) on \(E\) induced by the trivialization \(\phi\) is compatible with the Riemannian metric \(\langle, angle\) on \(E\) induced by the trivialization.

例 10.10. (Connection on \(T\mathbb{R}^n |_M\)). If \(M\) is a submanifold of \(\mathbb{R}^n\), the Euclidean metric on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) restricts to a Riemannian metric on the vector bundle \(T\mathbb{R}^n |_M\). Sections of the vector bundle \(T\mathbb{R}^n |_M\) are vector fields along \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). As noted in Example 10.2, the directional derivative in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) induces a connection

\(\displaystyle D : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M) ightarrow \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M) .\)

Proposition 4.10 asserts that the conneciton \(D\) on \(T\mathbb{R}^n |_M\) has zero curvature and is compatible with the metric.

The Gauss curvature equation for a surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), a key ingredient of the proof of Gauss's Theorema Egregium, is a consequence of the vanishing of the curvature tensor of the conneciton \(D\) on the bundle \(T\mathbb{R}^3 |_M\) (Theorem 8.1).

引理 10.11. Let \(E ightarrow M\) be a Riemannian bundle. Suppose \( abla^1, \ldots, abla^m\) are connections on \(E\) compatible with the metric and \(a_1, \ldots, a_m\) are \(C^{\infty}\) functions on \(M\) that add up to 1. Then \( abla = \sum_i a_i abla^i\) is a connection on \(E\) compatible with the metric.

证明. By Proposition 10.5, \( abla\) is a connection on \(E\). It remains to check that \( abla\) is compatible with the metirc. If \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(s, t \in \Gamma (E)\), then

\(\displaystyle X \langle s, t angle = \langle abla_X^i s, t angle + \langle s, abla_X^i t angle\)

for all \(i\) because \( abla^i\) is compatible with the metric. Now multiply (10.5) by \(a_i\) and sum

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} X \langle s, t angle & = \sum a_i X \langle s, t angle\ & = \left\langle \sum a_i abla_X^i s, t ight angle + \left\langle s, \sum a_i abla_X^i t ight angle\ & = \langle abla_X s, t angle + \langle s, abla_X t angle . \end{array}\)

\(\Box\)

命题 10.12. On any Riemannian bundle \(E ightarrow M\), there is a connection compatible with the metric.

10.6Restricting a Connection to an Open Subset

A connection \( abla\) on a vector bundle \(E\) over \(M\)

\(\displaystyle abla : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (E)\)

is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in the first argument, but not \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in the second argument. However, it turns out that the \(\mathcal{F}\)-linearity in the first argument and the Leibniz rule in the second argument are enough to imply that \( abla\) is a local operator.

命题 10.13. Let \( abla\) be a connection on a vector bundle \(E\) over a manifold \(M\), \(X\) be a smooth vector field on \(M\), and \(s\) a smooth section of \(E\). If either \(X\) or \(s\) vanishes identically on an open subset \(U\), then \( abla*X s\) vanishes identically on \(U\).

A conneciton on a vector bundle can be restricted to any open subset. Given a connection \( abla\) on a vector bundle \(E\), for every open set \(U\) there is a connection

\(\displaystyle abla^U : \mathfrak{X} (U) imes \Gamma (U, E) ightarrow \Gamma (U, E)\)

such that for any global vector field \( ar{X} \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and global section \( ar{s} \in \Gamma (E)\),

\(\displaystyle abla*{ ar{X} |_U}^U ( ar{s} |\_U) = ( abla_{ ar{X}} ar{s}) |_U .\)

Suppose \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (U)\) and \(s \in \Gamma (U, E)\). For any \(p \in U\), to define \( abla_X^U s \in \Gamma (U, E)\) first pick a global vector field \( ar{X}\) and a global section \( ar{s} \in \Gamma (E)\) that agrees with \(X\) and \(s\) in a neighborhood of \(p\). Then define

\(\displaystyle ( abla_X^U s) (p) = ( abla_{ ar{X}} ar{s}) (p) .\) (10.5)

Because \( abla*{ ar{X}} ar{s}\) is a local operator in \( ar{X}\) and in \( ar{s}\), this definition is independent of the choice of \( ar{X}\) and \( ar{s}\). It is a routing matter to show that \( abla^U\) satisfies all the properties of a connection on \(E |\_U\).

10.7Connections at a Point

Suppose \( abla\) is a connection on a vector bundle \(E\) over a manifold \(M\). For \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(s \in \Gamma (E)\), since \( abla_X s\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(X\), it is a point operator in \(X\) and Proposition 7.25 assures us that it can be defined pointwise in \(X\): there is a unique map, also denoted by \( abla\),

\(\displaystyle abla : T_p M imes \Gamma (E) ightarrow E_p\)

such that if \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(s \in \Gamma (E)\), then

\(\displaystyle abla*{X*p} s = ( abla_X s)\_p .\)

It is easy to check that \( abla*{X*p} s\) has the following properties: for \(X_p \in T_p M\) and \(s \in \Gamma (E)\),

  1. \( abla*{X*p} s\) is \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear in \(X_p\) and in \(s\);

  2. if \(f\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) function on \(M\), then

    \(\displaystyle abla*{X*p} (f s) = (X_p f) s (p) + f (p) abla*{X*p} s.\)

11Connection, Curvature, and Torsion Forms

根据高斯绝妙定理，如果 \(R_p\) 是 \(\mathbb{R}^3\) 中曲面 \(M\) 点 \(p\) 处的曲率张量，\(u, v\) 是切平面 \(T_p M\) 的任意正交标架，那么这点的高斯曲率是

\(\displaystyle K_p = \langle R_p (u, v) v, u angle .\) (11.1)

因为这个公式不依赖于曲面在 \(\mathbb{R}^3\) 中的嵌入，而仅依赖曲面的黎曼结构，它对任意2维黎曼流形有意义，可以作为这样一个曲面在一点处高斯曲率的定义，比如，双曲上半平面 \(\mathbb{H}^2\)。为了用 (11.1) 计算高斯曲率，我们需要先用含六项的公式 (6.8) 计算黎曼联络 \( abla\)，然后计算曲率张量 \(R_p (u, v) v\)，显然是一个非平凡的任务。

微分形式的一个重要优势就是它的可计算性，因此这一节我们用微分形式重新介绍联络、曲率和挠率。这会给下一节双曲上半平面的曲率带来简单的计算。

相对一个向量丛的标架，丛上的联络可以表示为1形式的矩阵，曲率为2形式的矩阵。这两个矩阵间的关系是联络的结构方程。

线性代数中的 Gram-Schmidt 过程把一个黎曼丛的任意 \(C^{\infty}\) 标架转为一个正交标架。相对这个正交标架，度量联络的联络矩阵是反对称的。根据结构方程，曲率矩阵也是反对称的。度量联络的曲率矩阵的反对称性在后面章节会有重要结果。

在切丛上，联络的挠率可以考试为2形式的矩阵，叫作挠率形式。有一个结构方程联系挠率形式和对偶形式以及联络形式。

11.1Connection and Curvature Forms

Let \( abla\) be a connection on a \(C^{\infty}\) rank \(r\) vector bundle \(\pi : E ightarrow M\). We are interested in describing \( abla\) locally. Section 10.6 shows how on every open subset \(U\) of \(M\), \( abla\) restricts to a connection on \(E |\_U ightarrow U\):

\(\displaystyle abla^U : \mathfrak{X} (U) imes \Gamma (U, E) ightarrow \Gamma (U, E) .\)

We will usually omit the superscript \(U\) and write \( abla^U\) as \( abla\).

Suppose \(U\) is a trivializing open set for \(E\) and \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\) is a frame for \(E\) over \(U\) (Proposition 7.22), and let \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) be a \(C^{\infty}\) vector field on \(U\). On \(U\), since any section \(s \in \Gamma (U, E)\) is a linear combination \(s = \sum a^j e_j\), the section \( abla_X s\) can be computed from \( abla_X e_j\) by linearity and the Leibniz rule. As a section of \(E\) over \(U\), \( abla_X e_j\) is a linear combination of the \(e_i\)'s with coefficients \(\omega_j^i\) depending on \(X\):

\(\displaystyle abla_X e_j = \sum \omega_j^i (X) e_i .\)

The \(\mathcal{F}\)-linearity of \( abla_X e_j\) in \(X\) implies that \(\omega_j^i\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(X\) and so \(\omega_j^i\) is a 1-form on \(U\) (Corollary 7.27). The 1-forms \(\omega_j^i\) on \(U\) are called the connection forms, and the matrix \(\omega = [\omega_j^i]\) is called the connection matrix, of the connection \( abla\) relative to the frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\) on \(U\).

Similarly, for \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), the section \(R (X, Y) e_j\) is a linear combination of \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\):

\(\displaystyle R (X, Y) e_j = \sum \Omega_j^i (X, Y) e_i .\)

Since

\(\displaystyle R (X, Y) = abla_X abla_Y - abla_Y abla_X - abla*{[X, Y]}\)

is alternating and is \(\mathcal{F}\)-bilinear, so is \(\Omega*j^i\). By Section 7.8, \(\Omega_j^i\) is a 2-form on \(U\). The 2-forms \(\Omega_j^i\) are called the curvature forms, and the matrix \(\Omega = [\Omega_j^i]\) is called the curvature matrix, of the connection \( abla\) relative to the frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\) on \(U\).

Recall that is \(\alpha, eta\) are \(C^{\infty}\) 1-forms and \(X, Y\) are \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields on a manifold, then

\(\displaystyle (\alpha \wedge eta) (X, Y) = \alpha (X) eta (Y) - \alpha (Y) eta (X)\) (11.2)

and

\(\displaystyle (d \alpha) (X, Y) = X \alpha (Y) - Y \alpha (X) - \alpha ([X, Y])\) (11.3)

定理 11.1. Let \( abla\) be a connection on a vector bundle \(E ightarrow M\) of rank \(r\). Relative to a frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\) for \(E\) over a trivializing open set \(U\), the curvature form \(\Omega_j^i\) are related to the connection forms \(\omega_j^i\) by the second structural equation:

\(\displaystyle \Omega_j^i = d \omega_j^i + \sum_k \omega_k^i \wedge \omega_j^k .\)

注意 11.2. (The Einstein summation convention). Einstein summation convention.

证明. (Theorem 11.1). Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be smooth vector fields on \(U\). Then

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} abla_X abla_Y e_j & = abla_X \sum_k (\omega_j^k (Y) e_k)\ & = \sum_k X \omega_j^k (Y) e_k + \sum_k \omega_j^k (Y) abla_X e_k\ & = \sum_i X \omega_j^i (Y) e_i + \sum*{i, k} \omega*j^k (Y) \omega_k^i (X) e_i . \end{array}\)

Interchanging \(X, Y\) gives

\(\displaystyle abla_Y abla_X e_j = \sum_i Y \omega_j^i (X) e_i + \sum*{i, k} \omega*j^k (X) \omega_k^i (Y) e_i .\)

Furthermore,

\(\displaystyle abla*{[X, Y]} e*j = \sum_i \omega_j^i ([X, Y]) e_i .\)

Hence, in Einstein notation,

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} R (X, Y) e_j = & abla_X abla_Y e_j - abla_Y abla_X e_j - abla*{[X, Y]} e_j\ = & (X \omega_j^i (Y) - Y \omega_j^i (X) - \omega_j^i ([X, Y])) e_i\ & + (\omega_k^i (X) \omega_j^k (Y) - \omega_k^i (Y) \omega_j^k (X)) e_i\ = & d \omega_j^i (X, Y) e_i + \omega_k^i \wedge \omega_j^k (X, Y) e_i \qquad (\operatorname{by} (11.3) \operatorname{and} (11.2))\ = & (d \omega*j^i + \omega_k^i \wedge \omega_j^k) (X, Y) e_i . \end{array}\)

Comparing this with the definition of the curvature form \(\Omega_j^i\) gives

\(\displaystyle \Omega_j^i = d \omega_j^i + \sum_k \omega_k^i \wedge \omega_j^k .\)
\(\Box\)

11.2Connections on a Framed Open Set

Suppose \(E\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundle over a manifold \(M\) and \(U\) is an open set on which there is a \(C^{\infty}\) frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\) for \(E\). We call \(U\) a framed open set for \(E\) for short. A connection \( abla\) on \(E |\_U\) determines a unique connection matrix \([\omega_j^i]\) relative to the frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\). Conversely, any matrix of 1-forms \([\omega_j^i]\) on \(U\) determines a connection on \(E |\_U\) as follows.

Given a matrix \([\omega_j^i]\) of 1-forms on \(U\), and \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (U)\), we set

\(\displaystyle abla_X e_j = \sum \omega_j^i (X) e_i,\)

and define \( abla_X Y\) by applying the Leibniz rule to \(Y = \sum h^j e_j\):

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} abla_X Y & = abla_X (h^j e_j) = (X h^j) e_j + h^j \omega_j^i (X) e_i\ & = ((X h^i) + h^j \omega_j^i (X)) e_i . \hspace{3cm} ext{(11.4)} \end{array}\)

With this definition, \( abla\) is a connection on \(E |\_U\).

11.3The Gram-Schmidt Process

线性代数里的 Gram-Schmidt 过程把内积空间 \(V\) 中的任意线性无关向量 \(v_1, \ldots, v_n\) 转为一个展成相同空间的正交向量集合。 \(\operatorname{proj}\_a b\) 表示把 \(b\) 正交投影到 \(a\) 的线性空间。那么,

\(\displaystyle \operatorname{proj}\_a b = rac{\langle b, a angle}{\langle a, a angle} a.\)

To carry out the Gram-Schimidt process, we first create an orthonormal set \(w_1, \ldots, w_n\):

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} w_1 & = v_1,\ w_2 & = v_2 -\operatorname{proj}*{w*1} v_2,\ & = v_2 - rac{\langle v_2, w_1 angle}{\langle w_1, w_1 angle} w_1,\ w_3 & = v_3 -\operatorname{proj}*{w*1} v_3 -\operatorname{proj}*{w_2} v_3 \hspace{3cm} ext{(11.5)}\ & = v_3 - rac{\langle v_3, w_1 angle}{\langle w_1, w_1 angle} w_1 - rac{\langle v_3, w_2 angle}{\langle w_2, w_2 angle} w_2, \end{array}\)

and so on.

命题 11.3. In the Gram-Schmidt process, for each \(k\), the set \(w_1, \ldots, w_k\) span the same linear subspace of \(V\) as \(v_1, \ldots, v_k\).

To get an orthonormal set \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\), define

\(\displaystyle e_i = rac{w_i}{\| w_i \|} .\) (11.6)

11.4Metric Connection Relative to an Orthonormal Frame

前面小节我们看到向量丛 \(E ightarrow M\) 的有标架的开集 \(U\) 上，一个联络完全由 \(U\) 上的1形式矩阵 \([\omega_j^i]\) 确定。假设向量丛 \(E ightarrow M\) 带有一个黎曼度量。度量联络的性质可以被翻译为正交标架上联络矩阵 \([\omega_j^i]\) 的条件。

命题 11.4. Let \(E ightarrow M\) be a Riemannian bundle and \( abla\) a connection on \(E\).

  1. If the connection \( abla\) is compatible with the metric, then its connection matrix \([\omega_j^i]\) relative to any orthonormal frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\) for \(E\) over a trivializing open set \(U \subset M\) is skew-symmetric.

  2. If every point \(p \in M\) has a trivializing neighborhood \(U\) for \(E\) such that the connection matrix \([\omega_j^i]\) relative to an orthonormal frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\) for \(E\) over \(U\) is skew-symmetric, then the connection \( abla\) is compatible with the metric.

证明.

  1. Suppose \( abla\) is compatible with the metric. For all \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(i, j\),

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} 0 = X \langle e*i, e_j angle & = \langle abla_X e_i, e_j angle + \langle e_i, abla_X e_j angle\ & = \langle \omega_i^k (X) e_k, e_j angle + \langle e_i, \omega_j^k (X) e_k angle\ & = \omega_i^k (X) \delta*{k j} + \omega_j^k (X) \delta i k\ & = \omega_i^j (X) + \omega_j^i (X) . \end{array}\)

    Hence,

    \(\displaystyle \omega_i^j = - \omega_j^i .\)

  2. We note first that compatibility with the metric is a local condition, so \( abla\) is compatible with the metric if and only if its restriction \( abla^U\) to any open set \(U\) is compatible with the metric. Suppose \(\omega_i^j = - \omega_j^i\). Let \(s = \sum a^i e_i\) and \(t = \sum b^j e_j\), with \(a^i, b^j \in C^{\infty} (U)\). Then

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} X \langle s, t angle & = X \left( \sum a^i b^i ight) = \sum (X a^i) b^i - \sum a^i X b^i .\ abla_X s & = abla_X (a^i e_i) = (X a^i) e_i + a^i abla_X e_i\ & = (X a^i) e_i + a^i \omega_i^k (X) e_k,\ \langle abla_X s, t angle & = \sum (X a^i) b^i + \sum a^i \omega_i^j (X) b^j \hspace{3cm} ext{(11.7)}\ \langle s, abla_X t angle & = \sum (X b^i) a^i + \sum b^i \omega_i^j (X) a^j\ & = \sum (X b^i) a^i + \sum a^i b^j \omega_j^i (X) . \hspace{3cm} ext{(11.8)} \end{array}\)

But by the skew-symmetric of \(\omega\),

\(\displaystyle \sum a^i b^j \omega_i^j (X) + \sum a^i b^j \omega_j^i (X) = 0.\)

Hence, adding (11.7) and (11.8) gives

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \langle abla_X s, t angle + \langle s, abla_X t angle & = \sum (X a^i) b^i + \sum (X b^i) a^i\ & = X \langle s, t angle . \end{array}\)

\(\Box\)

命题 11.5. If the connection matrix \([\omega_j^i]\) relative to a frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) of an affine connection on a manifold is skew-symmetric, then so is the curvature matrix \([\Omega_j^i]\).

证明. By the structural equation,

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \Omega_i^j & = d \omega_i^j + \omega_k^j \wedge \omega_i^k\ & = - d \omega_j^i + (- \omega_j^k) \wedge (- \omega_k^i)\ & = - d \omega_j^i - \omega_k^i \wedge \omega_j^k\ & = - \Omega_j^i . \end{array}\)

\(\Box\)

11.5Connections on the Tangent Bundle

A connection on the tangent bundle \(T M\) of a manifold \(M\) is simply an affine connection on \(M\). In addition to the curvature tensor \(R (X, Y ) Z\), an affine connection has a torsion tensor \(T (X, Y)\).

Let \(U\) be an open set in \(M\) on which the tangent bundle \(T M\) has a smooth frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\). If \(U\) is a coordinate open set with coordinates \(x^1, \ldots, x^n\), then \(\partial / \partial x^1, \ldots, \partial / \partial x^n\) is such a frame, but we will consider the more general setting where \(U\) need not be a coordinate open set. Let \( heta^1, \ldots, heta^n\) be the dual frame of 1-forms on \(U\); this means

\(\displaystyle heta^i (e_j) = \delta_j^i .\)

命题 11.6. If \(X\) is a smooth vector field on the open set \(U\), then \(X = \sum heta^i (X) e_i\).

For \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (U)\), the torsion \(T (X, Y)\) is a linear combination of the vector fields \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\), so we can write

\(\displaystyle T (X, Y) = \sum au^i (X, Y) e_i .\)

Since \(T (X, Y)\) is alternating and \(\mathcal{F}\)-bilinear, so are the coefficients \( au^i\). Therefore, the \({ au^i}' s\) are 2-forms on \(U\), called the torsion forms on the affine connection \( abla\) relative to the frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) on \(U\).

Since the torsion and curvature forms are determined completely by the frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) and the connection, there should be formulas for \( au^i\) and \(\Omega_j^i\) in terms of the dual forms and the connection forms. Indeed, Theorem 11.1 expresses the curvature forms in terms of the connection forms alone.

定理 11.7. (Structural equations).Relative to frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) for the tangent bundle over an open set \(U\) of a manifold \(M\), the torsion and curvature form of an affine connection on \(M\) can be given in terms of the dual 1-forms and the connection forms:

  1. (the first structural equation) \( au^i = d heta^i + \sum_j \omega_j^i \wedge heta^j\);

  2. (the second structural equation) \(\Omega_j^i = d \omega_j^i + \sum_k \omega_k^i \wedge \omega_j^k\).

证明. The second structural equation is a special case of Theorem 11.1, which is true more generally for any vector bundle. The proof of the first structural equation is a matter of unraveling the definition of the torsion. Let \(X, Y\) be smooth vector fields on \(U\). By Proposition 11.6, we can write

\(\displaystyle Y = \sum heta^j (Y) e_j .\)

Then

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} abla_X Y & = abla_X ( heta^j (Y) e_j)\ & = (X heta^j (Y)) e_j + heta^j (Y) abla_X e_j\ & = (X heta^j (Y)) e_j + heta^j (Y) \omega_j^i (X) e_i \end{array}\)

By symmetry,

\(\displaystyle abla_Y X = (Y heta^j (X)) e_j + heta^j (X) \omega_j^i (Y) e_i .\)

Finally, by Proposition 11.6 again,

\(\displaystyle [X, Y] = heta^i ([X, Y]) e_i .\)

Thus,

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} T (X, Y) & = abla_X Y - abla_Y X - [X, Y]\ & = ((X heta^i (Y) - Y heta^i (X) - heta^i ([X, Y]) ) + (\omega_j^i (X) heta^j (Y) - \omega_j^i (Y) heta^j (X)) ) e_i\ & = (d heta^i + \omega_j^i \wedge heta^j) (X, Y) e_i, \end{array}\)

where the last equality follows from (11.3) and (11.2). Hence,

\(\displaystyle au^i = d heta^i + \sum \omega_j^i \wedge heta^j .\)
\(\Box\)

我们现在可以把黎曼联络的定义的两个性质转为矩阵 \([\omega_j^i]\) 的条件。

命题 11.8. Let \(M\) be an Riemannian manifold and \(U\) an open subset on which there is an orthonormal frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\). Let \( heta^1, \ldots, heta^n\) be the dual frame of 1-forms. Then there exists a unique skew-symmetric matrix \([\omega_j^i]\) of 1-forms such that

\(\displaystyle d heta^i + \sum_j \omega_j^i \wedge heta^j = 0 \quad \operatorname{for}\operatorname{all}i = 1, \ldots, n.\) (11.9)

证明. In Theorem 6.6 we showed the existence of a Riemannian connection \( abla\) on any manifold. Let \([\omega_j^i]\) be the connection matrix of \( abla\) relative to the orthonormal frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) on \(U\). Because \( abla\) is compatible with the metric, by Proposition 11.4, the matrix \([\omega_j^i]\) is skew-symmetric. Because \( abla\) is torsion-free, by Theorem 11.7, it satisfies

\(\displaystyle d heta^i + \sum_j \omega_j^i \wedge heta^j = 0.\)

This proves the existence of the matrix \([\omega_j^i]\) with the two required properties.

To prove uniqueness, suppose \([\omega_j^i]\) is any skew-symmetric matrix of 1-forms on \(U\) satisfying (11.9). In Section 11.2, taking the vector bundle \(E\) to be the tangent bundle \(T M\), we showed that \([\omega_j^i]\) defines an affine connection \( abla\) on \(U\) of which it is the connection matrix relative to the frame \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\). Because \([\omega_j^i]\) is skew-symmetric, \( abla\) is compatible with the metric (Proposition 11.4), and because \([\omega_j^i]\) satisfies the equation (11.9), \( abla\) is torsion-free (Theorem 11.7). Thus, \( abla\) is the unique Riemannian connection on \(U\).\(\Box\)

If \(A = [\alpha_j^i]\) and \(B = [ eta_j^i]\) are matrices of differential forms on \(M\) with the number of columns of \(A\) equal to the number of rows of \(B\), then their wedge product \(A \wedge B\) is defined to be the matrix of differential forms whose \((i, j)\)-entry is

\(\displaystyle (A \wedge B)^i_j = \sum_k \alpha_k^i \wedge eta_j^k,\)

and \(d A\) is defined to be \([d \alpha_j^i]\). In matrix notation, we write

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{llll} au = \left[ egin{array}{c} au^1 \ \vdots\ au^n \end{array} ight], & heta = \left[ egin{array}{c} heta^1\ \vdots\ heta^n \end{array} ight], & \omega = [\omega_j^i], & \Omega = [\Omega_j^i] . \end{array}\)

Then the first structural equation becomes

\(\displaystyle au = d heta + \omega \wedge heta\)

and the second structural equation becomes

\(\displaystyle \Omega = d \omega + \omega \wedge \omega .\)

例. Connection and curvature forms on the Poincare disk

\(\displaystyle \mathbb{D}= \{ z = x + i y \in \mathbb{C}| | z | < 1 \}\)

in the complex plane with Riemannian metric

\(\displaystyle \langle, angle*z = rac{4 (d x \otimes d x + d y \otimes d y)}{(1 - | z |^2)^2} = rac{4 (d x \otimes d x + d y \otimes d y)}{(1 - x^2 - y^2)^2} .\)

An orthonormal frame for \(\mathbb{D}\) is

\(\displaystyle e_1 = rac{1}{2} (1 - | z |^2) rac{\partial}{\partial x}, \quad e_2 = rac{1}{2} (1 - | z |^2) rac{\partial}{\partial y} .\)

Find the connection matrix \(\omega = [\omega_j^i]\) and the curvature matrix \(\Omega = [\Omega_j^i]\) relative to the orthonormal frame \(e_1, e_2\) of the Riemannian connection on the Poincare disk. (Hint: first find the dual frame \( heta^1, heta^2\). Then solve for \(\omega_j^i\) in (11.9).)

12The Theorema Egregium Using Forms

第8节我们用向量场证明了高斯绝妙定理。这一节我们重新证明这个定理，但是用的是微分形式。一个关键步骤是高斯曲率公式的微分形式类比的推导。高斯绝妙定理给了曲面高斯曲率的内蕴性质，只依赖于度量而与欧氏空间中曲面的嵌入无关。这一特点可以用来定义抽象黎曼2流形的高斯曲率。我们以庞加莱半平面为例计算高斯曲率。

12.1The Gauss Curvature Equation

The Gauss curvature equation (Theorem 8.1) for an oriented surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) relates the curvature tensor to the shape operator. It has an analogue in terms of differential forms.

Consider a smooth surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) and a point \(p\) in \(M\). Let \(U\) be an open neighborhood of \(p\) in \(M\) on which there is an orthonormal frame \(e_1, e_2\). This is always possible by the Gram-Schimidt process, which turns any frame into an orthonormal frame. Let \(e_3\) be the cross product \(e_1 imes e_2\). Then \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) is an orthonormal frame for the vector bundle \(T\mathbb{R}^3 |\_U\) over \(U\).

For the connection \(D\) on the bundle \(T\mathbb{R}^3 |\_M\), let \([\omega_j^i]\) be the connection matrix of 1-forms relative to the orthonormal frame \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) over \(U\). Since \(D\) is compatible with the metric and the frame \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) is orthonormal, the matrix \([\omega_j^i]\) is skew-symmetric (Proposition 11.4). Hence, for \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\),

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{lllll} D_X e_1 = & & - \omega_2^1 (X) e_2 & - \omega_3^1 (X) e_3, & \hspace{3cm} ext{(12.1)}\ D_X e_2 = & \omega_2^1 (X) e_1 & & - \omega_3^2 (X) e*{3,} & \hspace{3cm} ext{(12.2)}\ D*X e_3 = & \omega_3^1 (X) e_1 & + \omega_3^2 (X) e_2 . & & \hspace{3cm} ext{(12.3)} \end{array}\)

Let \( abla\) be the Riemannian connection on the surface \(M\). Recall that for \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), the directional derivative \(D_X Y\) need not be tangent to the surface \(M\), and \( abla_X Y\) is simply the tangential component \((D_X Y)*{ an}\) of \(D*X Y\). By (12.1) and (12.2),

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{lll} abla_X e_1 = (D_X e_1)*{ an} = & - & \omega*2^1 (X) e_2,\ abla_X e_2 = (D_X e_2)*{ an} = & & \omega^1_2 (X) e_1 . \end{array}\)

It follows that the connection matrix of the Riemannian connection \( abla\) on \(M\) is

\(\displaystyle \omega = \left[ egin{array}{cc} 0 & \omega_2^1\ * \omega_2^1 & 0 \end{array} ight] = \left[ egin{array}{cc} 0 & 1\ - 1 & 0 \end{array} ight] \omega_2^1 .\)

Since

\(\displaystyle \omega \wedge \omega = \left[ egin{array}{cc} 0 & \omega_2^1\ * \omega_2^1 & 0 \end{array} ight] \wedge \left[ egin{array}{cc} 0 & \omega_2^1\ * \omega_2^1 & 0 \end{array} ight] = \left[ egin{array}{cc} * \omega_2^1 \wedge \omega_2^1 & 0\ 0 & - \omega_2^1 \wedge \omega_2^1 \end{array} ight] = \left[ egin{array}{cc} 0 & 0\ 0 & 0 \end{array} ight],\)

the curvature matrix of \( abla\) is

\(\displaystyle \Omega = d \omega + \omega \wedge \omega = d \omega = \left[ egin{array}{cc} 0 & d \omega_2^1\ * d \omega_2^1 & 0 \end{array} ight] = \left[ egin{array}{cc} 0 & 1\ * 1 & 0 \end{array} ight] d \omega_2^1 .\)

So the curvature matrix of \( abla\) is completely described by

\(\displaystyle \Omega_2^1 = d \omega_2^1 .\) (12.4)

Set the unit normal vector field \(N\) on \(U\) to be \(N = - e_3\). By (12.3), the shape operator \(L\) is described by

\(\displaystyle L (X) = - D_X N = D_X e_3 = \omega_3^1 (X) e_1 + \omega_3^2 (X) e_2, \quad X \in \mathfrak{X} (M) .\) (12.5)

定理 12.1. (Gauss curvature equation). Let \(e_1, e_2\) be an orthonormal frame of vector fields on an oriented open subset \(U\) of a surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), and let \(e_3\) be a unit normal vector field on \(U\). Relative to \(e_1, e_2, e_3\), the curvature form \(\Omega_2^1\) of the Riemannian connection on \(M\) is related to the connection forms of the directional derivative \(D\) on the bundle \(T\mathbb{R}^3 |\_M\) by

\(\displaystyle \Omega_2^1 = \omega_3^1 \wedge \omega_3^2 .\) (12.6)

We call formula (12.6) the Gauss curvature equation, because on the left-hand side, \(\Omega_2^1\) describes the curvature tensor of the surface, while on the right-hand side, \(\omega_3^1\) and \(\omega_3^2\) describe the shape operator.

证明. Let \( ilde{\Omega}\_j^i\) be the curvature forms of the connection \(D\) on \(T\mathbb{R}^3 |\_M\). Because the curvature tensor of \(D\) is zero, the secodn structural equation for \( ilde{\Omega}\) gives

\(\displaystyle ilde{\Omega}\_j^i = d \omega_j^i + \sum_k \omega_k^i \wedge \omega_j^k = 0.\) (12.7)

In particular,

\(\displaystyle d \omega_2^1 + \omega^1_1 \wedge \omega^1_2 + \omega^1_2 \wedge \omega^2_2 + \omega^1_3 \wedge \omega^3_2 = 0.\)

Since \(\omega^1_1 = \omega^2_2 = 0\), this reduces to

\(\displaystyle d \omega^1_2 + \omega^1_3 \wedge \omega^3_2 = 0.\)

Since the matrix \([\omega^i_j]\) is skew-symmetric,

\(\displaystyle d \omega^1_2 = \omega^1_3 \wedge \omega^2_3 .\)

The Gauss curvature equation now follows from (12.4).\(\Box\)

12.2The Theorema Egregium

We will derive formulas for the Gaussian curvature of a surface in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), first in terms of the connection forms for the directional derivative and then in terms of the curvature form \(\Omega^1_2\).

命题 12.2. For a smooth surface in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), if \(e_1, e_2\) is an orthonormal frame over an oriented open subset \(U\) of the surface and \(e_3\) is a unit normal vector field on \(U\), then the Gaussian curvature \(K\) on \(U\) is given by

\(\displaystyle K = \det \left[ egin{array}{cc} \omega^1_3 (e_1) & \omega^1_3 (e_2)\ \omega^2_3 (e_1) & \omega^2_3 (e_2) \end{array} ight] = (\omega^1_3 \wedge \omega^2_3) (e_1, e_2) .\)

证明. From (12.5),

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{l} L (e_1) = \omega^1_3 (e_1) e_1 + \omega^2_3 (e_1) e_2,\ L (e_2) = \omega^1_3 (e_2) e_1 + \omega^2_3 (e_2) e_2 . \end{array}\)

So the matrix of \(L\) relative to the frame \(e_1, e_2\) is

\(\displaystyle \left[ egin{array}{cc} \omega^1_3 (e_1) & \omega^1_3 (e_2)\ \omega^2_3 (e_1) & \omega^2_3 (e_2) \end{array} ight] .\)

Therefore,

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{ll} K & = \det L = \det \left[ egin{array}{cc} \omega^1_3 (e_1) & \omega^1_3 (e_2)\ \omega^2_3 (e_1) & \omega^2_3 (e_2) \end{array} ight]\ & = \omega^1_3 (e_1) \omega^2_3 (e_2) - \omega^1_3 (e_2) \omega^2_3 (e_1) = \left( \omega^1_3 {\wedge \omega^2_3} ight) (e_1, e_2) . \end{array}\)

\(\Box\)

定理 12.3. (Theorema Egregium). For a smooth surface in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), if \(e*1, e_2\) is an orthonormal frame over an open subset \(U\) of the surface with dual frame \( heta^1, heta^2\), then the Gaussian curvature \(K\) on \(U\) is given by

\(\displaystyle K = \Omega^1_2 (e_1, e_2)\) (12.8)

or by

\(\displaystyle d \omega^1_2 = K heta^1 \wedge heta^2 .\) (12.9)

证明. Formula (12.8) is an immediate consequence of Proposition 12.2 and the Gauss curvature equation (12.6).

As for (12.9), since

\(\displaystyle K = K ( heta^1 \wedge heta^2) (e_1, e_2) = \Omega^1_2 (e_1, e_2)\)

and a 2-form on \(U\) is completely determined by its value on \(e_1, e_2\), we have \(\Omega^1_2 = K heta^1 \wedge heta^2\). By (12.4),

\(\displaystyle d \omega^1_2 = K heta^1 \wedge heta^2 .\)
\(\Box\)

By the definition of the curvature matrix, if \(e_1, e_2\) is an orthonormal frame on an open subset \(U\) of \(M\), then

\(\displaystyle R (X, Y) e_2 = \Omega^1_2 (X, Y) e_1 \quad \operatorname{for}\operatorname{all}X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (U) .\)

so that

\(\displaystyle \langle R (e_1, e_2) e_2, e_1 angle = \langle \Omega^1_2 (e_1, e_2) e_1, e_1 angle = \Omega^1_2 (e_1, e_2) .\)

定义 12.4. The Gaussian curvature K at a point \(p\) of a Riemannian 2-manifold \(M\) is defined to be

\(\displaystyle K_p = \langle R_p (u, v) v, u angle\) (12.10)

for any orthonormal basis \(u, v\) for the tangent plane \(T_p M\).

为了让高斯曲率定义有效，我们需要证明公式(12.10)不依赖于正交基的选择。我们在下一小节证明。

12.3Skew-Symmetric of the Curvature Tensor

Recall that the curvature of an affine connection \( abla\) on a manifold \(M\) is defined to be

\(\displaystyle R : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \mathfrak{X} (M) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (M),\)
\(\displaystyle R (X, Y) Z = abla_X abla_Y Z - abla_Y abla_X Z - abla*{[X, Y]} Z.\)

We showed that \(R (X, Y) Z\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in every argument; therefore, it is a point operator. A point operator is also called a tensor. It is immediate from the definition that the curvature tensor \(R (X, Y) Z\) is skew-symmetric in \(X\) and \(Y\).

命题 12.5. If an affine connection \( abla\) on a Riemannian manifold \(M\) is compatible with the metric, then for vector fields \(X, Y, Z, W \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), the tensor \(\langle R (X, Y) Z, W angle\) is skew-symmetric in \(Z\) and \(W\).

We now show that \(\langle R (u, v) v, u angle\) is independent of the orthonormal basis \(u, v\) for \(T*p M\). Suppose \( ar{u}, ar{v}\) is another orthonormal basis. Then

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} ar{u} & = a u + b v,\ ar{v} & = c u + d v \end{array}\)

for an orthonormal matrix \(A = \left[ egin{array}{cc} a & b\ c & d \end{array} ight]\), and

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \langle R ( ar{u}, ar{v}) ar{v}, ar{u} angle & = \langle (\det A) R (u, v) (c u + d v), a u + b v angle\ & = (\det A)^2 \langle R (u, v) v, u angle \end{array}\)

by the skew-symmetry of \(\langle R (u, v) z, w angle\) in \(z\) and \(w\). Since \(A \in O (2)\), \(\det A = \pm 1\). Hence,

\(\displaystyle \langle R ( ar{u}, ar{v}) ar{v}, ar{u} angle = \langle R (u, v) v, u angle .\)

12.4Sectional Curvarture

Let \(M\) be a Riemannian manifold and \(p\) a point in \(M\). If \(P\) is a 2-dimensional subspace of the tangent spacce \(T_p M\), then we define the sectional curvature of \(P\) to be

\(\displaystyle K (P) = \langle R (e_1, e_2) e_2, e_1 angle\) (12.11)

for any orthonormal basis \(e_1, e_2\) of \(P\). Just as in the definition of the Gaussian curvature, the right-hand side of (12.11) is independent of the orthonormal basis \(e_1, e_2\).

If \(u, v\) is an arbitrary basis for the 2-plane \(P\), then a computation similar to that in Section 8.3 shows that the sectional curvature of \(P\) is also given by

\(\displaystyle K (P) = rac{\langle R (u, v) v, u angle}{\langle u, u angle \langle v, v angle - \langle u, v angle^2} .\)

12.5Poincaré Half-Plane

例 12.6. (The Gaussian curvature of the Poincaré half-plane). The Poincaré half-plane is the upper half-plane

\(\displaystyle \mathbb{H}^2 = \{ (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 |y > 0 \}\)

with the metric

\(\displaystyle \langle, angle*{(x, y)} = rac{d x \otimes d x + d y \otimes d y}{y^2} .\)

Classically, the notation for a Riemannian metric is \(d s^2\). Hence, the metric on the Poincaré half-plane is

\(\displaystyle d s^2 = rac{d x \otimes d x + d y \otimes d y}{y^2} .\)

With this metric, an orthonormal frame is

\(\displaystyle e_1 = y rac{\partial}{\partial x}, \quad e_2 = y rac{\partial}{\partial y} .\)

So the dual frame is

\(\displaystyle heta^1 = rac{1}{y} d x, \quad heta^2 = rac{1}{y} d y.\)

Hence,

\(\displaystyle d heta^1 = rac{1}{y^2} d x \wedge d y, \quad d heta^2 = 0.\) (12.12)

On the Poincare half-plane the connection form \(\omega^1_2\) is a linear combination of \(d x\) and \(d y\), so we may write

\(\displaystyle \omega^1_2 = a d x + b d y.\) (12.13)

We will determine the coefficients \(a, b\) from the first structural equation:

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} d heta^1 = & - \omega^1_2 \wedge heta^2, \hspace{3cm} ext{(12.14)}\ d heta^2 = & - \omega^2_1 \wedge heta^1 = \omega^1_2 \wedge heta^1 . \hspace{3cm} ext{(12.15)} \end{array}\)

By (12.12), (12.13) and (12.14),

\(\displaystyle rac{1}{y^2} d x \wedge d y = d heta^1 = - (a d x + b d y) \wedge rac{1}{y} d y = - rac{a}{y} d x \wedge d y.\)

So \(a = - 1 / y\). By (12.12), (12.13) and (12.15),

\(\displaystyle 0 = d heta^2 = \left( - rac{1}{y} d x + b d y ight) \wedge rac{1}{y} d x = - rac{b}{y} d x \wedge d y.\)

So \(b = 0\). Therefore,

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \omega^1_2 & = - rac{1}{y} d x,\ d \omega^1_2 & = rac{1}{y^2} d y \wedge d x\ & = - rac{1}{y^2} d x \wedge d y. \end{array}\)

By definition, the Gaussian curvature of the Poincare half-plane is

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} K & = \Omega^1_2 (e_1, e_2) = - rac{1}{y^2} (d x \wedge d y) \left( y rac{\partial}{\partial x}, y rac{\partial}{\partial y} ight)\ & = - (d x \wedge d y) \left( rac{\partial}{\partial x}, rac{\partial}{\partial y} ight) = - 1. \end{array}\)

例. The orthogonal group \(O (2)\)

  1. Show that an element \(A\) of \(O (2)\) is either

    \(\displaystyle A = \left[ egin{array}{cc} a & - b\ b & a \end{array} ight] \quad \operatorname{or} \quad \left[ egin{array}{cc} a & b\ b & - a \end{array} ight],\)

    where \(a^2 + b^2 = 1, a, b \in \mathbb{R}\).

  2. Let \(\operatorname{SO} (2) = \{ A \in O (2) | \det A = 1 \}\). Show that every element \(A\) of SO(2) is of the form

    \(\displaystyle A = \left[ egin{array}{cc} \cos t & - \sin t\ \sin t & \cos t \end{array} ight], \quad t \in \mathbb{R}.\)

    Thus, \(\operatorname{SO} (2)\) is the group of rotations about the origin in \(\mathbb{R}^2\).

Let \(J = \left[ egin{array}{cc} 1 & 0\ 0 & - 1 \end{array} ight]\). Then \(O (2) =\operatorname{SO} (2) \cup \operatorname{SO} (2) J\). This proves that \(O (2)\) has two connected components, each diffeomorphic to the circle \(S^1\).
entry.summary: chapter 2 of Differential Geometry, Connection, Curvature and Characteristic Classes

entry.title: Curvature and Vector Fields
entry.content:
1Curvature and Vector Fields

流形上的光滑映射，微分，向量场和微分形式，是流形的微分拓扑。这本书注重在微分几何。现在流形会被赋予一个黎曼度量，提供一种测量长度的方式。

1827年高斯在 General investigation of curved surfaces 中打下了微分几何的基础。他的伟大成就之一是证明了保距离映射下高斯曲率的不变性。这一结果被成为Gauss's Theorema Egregium，在拉丁文中是 “remarkable theorem”。用绝妙定理，我们可以用高斯曲率判断非等距曲面。这本书的前八章的目标是介绍足够的微分几何的基本构造来证明绝妙定理。

1Riemannian Manifolds

这章回忆一些向量空间内积的概念，用单位分解的方法证明流形上黎曼度量的存在性。

1.1Inner Product on a Vector Space

A point \(u\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) \((u^1, u^2, u^3)\) or

\(\displaystyle \left[ egin{array}{c} u^1 \ u^2\ u^3 \end{array} ight] .\)

The Euclidean inner product, or the dot product, is defined by

\(\displaystyle \langle u, v angle = \sum_{i = 1}^3 u^i v^i .\)

The length of a vector

\(\displaystyle \| v \| = \sqrt{\langle v, v angle},\) (1.1)

the angle \( heta\) between two nonzero vectors

\(\displaystyle \cos heta = rac{\langle u, v angle}{\| u \| \| v \|}, \quad 0 \leqslant heta \leqslant \pi,\) (1.2)

the arc length of a parametrized curve \(c (t)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), \(a \leqslant t \leqslant b\),

\(\displaystyle s = \int_a^b \| c' (t) \| d t.\)

定义 1.1. An inner product on a real vector space \(V\) is a positive-definite, symmetric, bilinear form \(\langle, angle : V imes V ightarrow \mathbb{R}\).

  1. (positive-definiteness) \(\langle v, v angle \geqslant 0\); the equality holds if and only if \(v = 0\).

  2. (symmetry) \(\langle u, v angle = \langle v, u angle\)

  3. (bilinearity) \(\langle a u + b v, w angle = a \langle u, w angle + b \langle v, w angle\)

命题 1.2. (Restriction of an inner product to a subspace). Let \(\langle, angle\) be an inner product on a vector space \(V\). If \(W\) is a subspace of \(V\), then the restriction

\(\displaystyle \langle, angle_W := \langle, angle |_{W imes W} : W imes W ightarrow \mathbb{R}\)

is an inner product on \(W\).

命题 1.3. (Nonnegative linear combination of inner products). Let \(a_1, \ldots, a_r\) be nonnegative real numbers with at least one \(a_i > 0\). The linear combination \(\langle, angle := \sum a_i \langle, angle_i\) is again an inner product.

1.2Representation of Inner Products by Symmetric Matrices

Let \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) be a basis of a vector space \(V\).

\(\displaystyle \sum x^i e_i \longleftrightarrow \mathbf{x}= \left[ egin{array}{c} x^1\ \vdots\ x^n \end{array} ight], \quad \sum y^i e_i \longleftrightarrow \mathbf{y}= \left[ egin{array}{c} y^1\ \vdots\ y^n \end{array} ight] .\)

Let \(A\) be an \(n imes n\) matrix whose entries are

\(\displaystyle a_{i j} = \langle e_i, e_j angle .\)
\(\displaystyle \left\langle \sum x^i e_i, \sum y^j e_j ight angle = \sum a_{i j} x^i y^j =\mathbf{x}^T A\mathbf{y}.\)

定义 1.4. An \(n imes n\) symmetric matrix \(A\) is said to be positive-definite if

  1. \(\mathbf{x}^T A\mathbf{x} \geqslant 0\) for all \(x\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\),

  2. equality holds if and only if \(\mathbf{x}= 0\).

There is a one-to-one correspondence:

inner product on a vector space \(\leftrightarrow\) \(n imes n\) positive definite symmetric matrix.

The dual space \(V^{\vee}\) of a vector space \(V\) is by definition \(\operatorname{Hom} (V, \mathbb{R})\), the space of all linear maps from \(V\) to \(\mathbb{R}\). Let \(\alpha^1, \ldots, \alpha^n\) be the basis for \(V^{\vee}\), then \(\alpha^i (x) = x^i\).

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \langle x, y angle & = \sum a_{i j} x^i y^j = \sum a_{i j} \alpha^i (x) \alpha^j (y)\ & = \sum a_{i j} (\alpha^i \otimes \alpha^j) (x, y) . \end{array}\)

In terms of tensor product, an inner product can be written as

\(\displaystyle \langle, angle = \sum a_{i j} \alpha^i \otimes \alpha^j .\)

1.3Riemannian Metrics

定义 1.5. A Riemannian metric on a manifold \(M\) is the assignment to each point \(p\) in \(M\) of an inner product \(\langle, angle_p\) on the tangent space \(T_p M\); the assignment is required to be \(C^{\infty}\) in the following sense: if \(X\) and \(Y\) are \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields on \(M\), then \(p \mapsto \langle X_p, Y_p angle_p\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) function on \(M\). A Riemannian manifold is a pair \(\langle M, \langle, angle angle\).

例 1.6. The Euclidean inner product on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) give rise to a Riemannian metric on \(\mathbb{R}^n\), called the Euclidean metric on \(\mathbb{R}^n\).

例 1.7. A submanifold \(M\) of a manifold \(N\) is said to be regular if locally it is defined by the vanishing of a set of coordinates. Locally, a regular submanifold looks like a \(k\)-plane in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). At each point \(p\) in \(M\), the tangent space \(T_p M\) is a vector subspace of \(T_p \mathbb{R}^3\). The Euclidean metric on \(\mathbb{R}^3\) restricts to a function

\(\displaystyle \langle, angle_M : T_p M imes T_p M ightarrow \mathbb{R},\)

which is clearly positive-definite, symmetric, and bilinear. Thus a surface in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) inherits a Riemannian metric from the Euclidean metric on \(\mathbb{R}^3\).

定义 1.8. A \(C^{\infty}\) map \(F : (N, \langle, angle') ightarrow (M, \langle, angle)\) of Riemannian manifolds is said to be metric-preserving if for all \(p \in N\) and tangent vectors \(u, v \in T_p N\),

\(\displaystyle \langle u, v angle_p' = \langle F_{\ast} u, F_{\ast} v angle_{F (p)} .\) (1.3)

An isometry is a metric-preserving diffeomorphism.

例 1.9. If \(F : N ightarrow M\) is a diffeomorphism and \(\langle, angle\) is a Riemannian metric on \(M\), then (1.3) defines an induced Riemannian metric \(\langle, angle'\) on \(N\).

例 1.10. Let \(N\) and \(M\) be the unit circle in \(\mathbb{C}\). Define \(F : N ightarrow M\), a 2-sheeted covering space map, by \(F (z) = z^2\). Give \(M\) a Riemannian metric \(\langle, angle\), for example the Euclidean metric as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}^2\), and define \(\langle, angle'\) on \(N\) by

\(\displaystyle \langle v, w angle' = \langle F_{\ast} v, F_{\ast} w angle .\)

Then \(\langle, angle'\) is a Riemannian metric on \(N\). The map \(F\) is metric-preserving but not an isometry because \(F\) is not a diffeomorphism.

例 1.11. A torus in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) inherits the Euclidean metric from \(\mathbb{R}^3\). However, a torus is also the quotient space of \(\mathbb{R}^2\) by the group \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) acting as translations, or to put it more plainly, the quotient space of a square with the opposite edges identified. In this way, it inherits a Riemannian metric from \(\mathbb{R}^2\). With these two metrics, the torus becomes two different Riemannian manifolds. We will show later that there is no isometry between these two Riemannian manifolds with the same underlying torus.

1.4Existence of a Riemannian Metric

We will write \(\partial_i\) for the coordinate vector field \(\partial / \partial x^i\).

\(\displaystyle \langle X, Y angle = \sum a^i b^i\) (1.4)

defines a Riemannian metric on \(U\).

To construct a Riemannian metric on \(M\) one needs to piece together the Riemannian metrics on the various coordinate open sets of an atlas.

A collection \(\{ S_{\alpha} \}\) of subsets of a topological space \(S\) is said to be locally finite if every point \(p \in S\) has a neighborhood \(U_p\) that intersects only finitely many of the subsets \(S_{\alpha}\). The support of a function \(f : S ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) is the closure of the subset of \(S\) on which \(f eq 0\):

\(\displaystyle \operatorname{supp}f =\operatorname{cl} \{ x \in S|f (x) eq 0 \} .\)

Suppose \(\{ U_{\alpha} \}_{\alpha \in A}\) is an open cover of a manifold \(M\). A collection of nonnegative \(C^{\infty}\) functions

\(\displaystyle ho_{\alpha} : M ightarrow \mathbb{R}, \quad \alpha \in A\)

is called a \(C^{\infty}\) partition of unity subordinate to \(\{ U_{\alpha} \}\) if

  1. \(\operatorname{supp} ho_{\alpha} \subset U_{\alpha}\) for all \(\alpha\),

  2. the collection of supports, \(\{ \operatorname{supp} ho_{\alpha} \}_{\alpha \in A}\) is locally finite,

  3. \(\sum_{\alpha \in A} ho_{\alpha} = 1\).

定理 1.12. On every manifold \(M\) there is a Riemannian metric.

2Curves

A parametrized curve, a smooth map \(c : [a, b] ightarrow M\), or it is the set of points in \(M\) that is the image of this map. A regular curve is a parametrized curve whose velocity is never zero.

2.1Regular Curves

定义 2.1. A parametrized curve \(c : [a, b] ightarrow M\) is regular if its velocity \(c' (t)\) is never zero for all \(t\) in the domain \([a, b]\). In other words, a regular curve in \(M\) is an immersion: \([a, b] ightarrow M\).

例 2.2. The curve \(c : [- 1, 1] ightarrow \mathbb{R}^2\),

\(\displaystyle c (t) = (t^3, t^2),\)

is not regular at \(t = 0\).

Among the various reparametrizations of a regular curve, the most important is the arc length parameterization.

2.2Arc Length Parameterization

The arc length of the curve to be

\(\displaystyle \ell = \int_a^b \| c' (u) \| d u.\)
\(\displaystyle s (t) = \int_a^t \| c' (u) \| d t.\)

The function \(s : [a, b] ightarrow [0, \ell]\) is the arc length function of the curve.

命题 2.3. The arc length function \(s : [a, b] ightarrow [0, \ell]\) of a regular curve \(c : [a, b] ightarrow M\) has a \(C^{\infty}\) inverse.

We can write \(t\) as a \(C^{\infty}\) function of the arc length to get the arc length parametrization \(\gamma (s) = c (t (s))\).

命题 2.4. A curve is parametrized by arc length if and only if it has unit speed and its parameter starts at 0.

例 2.5. The curve \(c : [0, 2 \pi] ightarrow \mathbb{R}^2\),

\(\displaystyle c (t) = (a \cos t, a \sin t), \quad a > 0,\)

is regular. Its image is the circle of radius \(a\) centered at the origin. Its arc length function is

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} s & = \int_0^t \| c' (u) \| d u = \int_0^t \left\| \left[ egin{array}{c} - a \sin u\ a \cos u \end{array} ight] ight\| d u\ & = \int_0^t a d u = a t. \end{array}\)

Hence, \(t = s / a\) and the arc length parametrization is

\(\displaystyle \gamma (s) = \left( a \cos rac{s}{a}, a \sin rac{s}{a} ight) .\)

2.3Signed Curvature of a Plane Curve

We will quantify the notion of curvature for a curve in the plane \(\mathbb{R}^2\).

Ou plane curve \(\gamma : [0, \ell] ightarrow \mathbb{R}^2\) will be parameterized by the arc length \(s\). Then the velocity vector \(T (s) = \gamma' (s)\) has unit length and is tangent to the curve at the point \(p = \gamma (s)\). A reasonable measure of curvature at \(p\) is the magnitude of the derivative

\(\displaystyle T' (s) = rac{d T}{d s} (s) = \gamma'' (s),\)

since the faster \(T\) changes, the more the curve bends. However, in order to distinguish the directions in which the curve can bend, we will define a curvature with a sign. Usually \(\mathbf{n} (s)\) is chosen so that the pair \((T (s), \mathbf{n} (s))\) is oriented positively in the plane, i.e., counterclockwise.

\(\displaystyle \langle T, T angle = 1\)

Differentiate this equation with respect to \(s\) gives

\(\displaystyle \langle T', T angle + \langle T, T' angle = 0\)
\(\displaystyle \langle T', T angle = 0\)

Thus, \(T'\) is perpendicular to \(T\) and so it must be a multiple of \(\mathbf{n}\). The scalar \(\kappa\) such that

\(\displaystyle T' = \kappa \mathbf{n}\)

is called the signed curvature. We can also write

\(\displaystyle \kappa = \langle T', \mathbf{n} angle = \langle \gamma'', \mathbf{n} angle .\)

例 2.6. (The circle).

\(\displaystyle \gamma (s) = \left( a \cos rac{s}{a}, a \sin rac{s}{a} ight), \quad 0 \leq s \leq 2 \pi a.\)
\(\displaystyle T (s) = \gamma' (s) = \left[ egin{array}{c} - \sin rac{s}{a}\ \cos rac{s}{a} \end{array} ight],\)
\(\displaystyle T' (s) = \gamma'' (s) = \left[ egin{array}{c} - rac{1}{a} \cos rac{s}{a}\ - rac{1}{a} \sin rac{s}{a} \end{array} ight] .\)
\(\displaystyle \operatorname{rot} \left( rac{\pi}{2} ight) = \left[ egin{array}{cc} \cos rac{\pi}{2} & - \sin rac{\pi}{2}\ \sin rac{\pi}{2} & \cos rac{\pi}{2} \end{array} ight] = \left[ egin{array}{cc} 0 & - 1\ 1 & 0 \end{array} ight]\)
\(\displaystyle \mathbf{n}= \left[ egin{array}{cc} 0 & - 1\ 1 & 0 \end{array} ight] T = \left[ egin{array}{c} - \cos rac{s}{a}\ - \sin rac{s}{a} \end{array} ight]\)

So \(T' = (1 / a) \mathbf{n}\) and \(\kappa (s) = 1 / a\).

2.4Orientation and Curvature

Reversing the orientation of a plane curve reverses the sign of its signed curvature at any point.

例 2.7. The counterclockwise circle

\(\displaystyle \gamma (s) = (a \cos s / a, a \sin s / a), \quad 0 \leq s \leq 2 \pi a\)

has signed curvature \(1 / a\), and the clockwise circle

\(\displaystyle ilde{\gamma} (s) = \gamma (- s)\)

has signed curvature \(- 1 / a\).

例. The Frenet-Serret formulas

Let \(\gamma : I ightarrow \mathbb{R}^3\) be a regular space curve parameterized by arc length. Then \(T = \gamma' (s)\) is tangent to \(\gamma\) at \(\gamma (s)\) and has unit length. Assume that \(\gamma'' (s) eq 0\).

  1. Prove that \(\gamma'' (s)\) is normal to \(T\).

  2. Let \(N\) be the unit vector \(\gamma'' (s) / \| \gamma'' (s) \|\). Then \(T' = \kappa N\), where \(\kappa\) is the curvature of the space curve.

  3. The unit vector \(B = T imes N\) is called the binormal of \(\gamma\) at \(\gamma (s)\). The three vectors \(T, N, B\) form an orhonormal basis for \(\mathbb{R}^3\) at \(\gamma (s)\), called the Frenet-Serret frame. Prove that

    \(\displaystyle N' = - \kappa T + au B\)

    for some real number \( au\), which is called the torsion of the unit-speed curve \(\gamma\).

  4. Prove that \(B' = - au N\).

    The set of equations

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} T' = & \kappa N\ N' = & - \kappa T + au B\ B' = & - au N \end{array}\)

    is called the Frenet-Serret formulas. In matrix notation

    \(\displaystyle \left[ egin{array}{c} T\ N\ B \end{array} ight]' = \left[ egin{array}{ccc} & \kappa & \ - \kappa & & au\ & - au & \end{array} ight] \left[ egin{array}{c} T\ N\ B \end{array} ight] . \hspace{10em} \operatorname{vg}\)

3Surfaces in Space

3.1Principal, Mean, and Gaussian Curvatures

There are several ways to generalize the curvature of a plane curve to a surface. One way, following Euler, is to consider the curvature of all the normal sections of the surface at a point. A second way is to study the derivative of a unit normal vector field on the surface. This section uses Euler's method to define several measurements of curvature at a point on a surface. We then state two theorems, Gauss's Theorema Egregium and the Gauss-Bonnet theorem. We will take up the relationship between curvature and the derivative of a normal vector field in Section 5.

3.2Principal, Mean, and Gaussian Curvatures

By a surface in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), we mean a 2-dimensional regular submanifold of \(\mathbb{R}^3\). A normal vector to \(M\) at \(p\) is a vector \(N_p \in T_p \mathbb{R}^3\) that is orthogonal to the tangent plane \(T_p M\). A normal vector field on \(M\) is a function \(N\) that assigns to each \(p \in M\) a normal vector \(N_p\) at \(p\). If \(N\) is a normal vector field on \(M\), then at each point \(p \in M\), we can write

\(\displaystyle N_p = \sum_{i = 1}^3 a^i (p) rac{\partial}{\partial x^i} |_p .\)

The normal vector field \(N\) on \(M\) is said to be \(C^{\infty}\) if the coefficient functions \(a^1, a^2, a^3\) are \(C^{\infty}\) functions on \(M\).

Let \(N\) be a \(C^{\infty}\) unit normal vector field on a neighborhood of \(p\) in \(M\). Denote by \(N_p\) the unit normal vector at \(p\). Under the canonical identification of \(T_p \mathbb{R}^3\) with \(\mathbb{R}^3\), every plane \(P\) through \(N_p\) slices the surface \(M\) along a plane curve \(P \cap M\) through \(p\). By the transversality theorem from differential topology, the intersection \(P \cap M\), being transversal, is smooth. We call such a plane curve a normal section of the surface through \(p\). We can compute the curvature of a normal section with respect to \(N_p\). The collection of the curvatures at \(p\) of all the normal sections gives a fairly good picture of how the surface curves at \(p\).

More precisely, each unit tangent vector \(X_p\) to the surface \(M\) at \(p\) determines together with \(N_p\) a plane that slices \(M\) along a normal section. The unit tangent vector \(X_p\) determines an orientation of the normal section. Let \(\gamma (s)\) be the arc length parameterization of this normal section with initial point \(\gamma (0) = p\) and intial vector \(\gamma' (0) = X_p\). Note that \(\gamma (0)\) is completely determined by the unit tangent vector \(X_p\). Define the normal curvature of the normal section \(\gamma (s)\) at \(p\) with respect to \(N_p\) by

\(\displaystyle \kappa (X_p) = \langle \gamma'' (0), N_p angle\) (3.1)

Of course, the \(N_p\) is not always the same as the \(\mathbf{n} (0)\) in Section 2, which was obtained by rotating the unit tangent vector \(90^{\circ}\) counterclockwise in \(\mathbb{R}^2\); an arbitrary plane in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) does not have a preferred orientation.

Since the set of all unit vectors in \(T_p M\) is a circle, we have a function

\(\displaystyle \kappa : S^1 ightarrow \mathbb{R}.\)

\(\kappa (- X_p) = \kappa (X_p)\) for \(X_p \in S^1\).

The maximum and minimum values \(\kappa_1, \kappa_2\) of the function \(\kappa\) are the principal curvatures of the surface at \(p\). Their average \((\kappa_1 + \kappa_2) / 2\) is the mean curvature \(H\), and their product \(\kappa_1 \kappa_2\) the Gaussian curvature \(K\). A unit direction \(X_p \in T_p M\) along which a principal curvature occurs is called a principal direction. Note that if \(X_p\) is a principal direction, then so is \(- X_p\), since \(\kappa (- X_p) = \kappa (X_p)\). If \(\kappa_1\) and \(\kappa_2\) are equal, then every unit vector in \(T_p M\) is a principal direction.

注意 3.1. Using \(- N_p\) instead of \(N_p\) reverses the sign of all the normal curvatures at \(p\). This will change the sign of the mean curv, but not the Gaussian curv.

例 3.2. (Sphere of radius \(a\)). \(1 / a\), \(H = 1 / a, K = 1 / a^2\).

例 3.3. For a plane \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) the principal curvatures, mean curvature and Gaussian curvature are all zero.

例 3.4. For a cylinder of radius \(a\) with a unit inward normal, it appears that the principal curvatures are 0 and \(1 / a\). The mean curvature is \(1 / 2 a\), the Gaussian curvature is 0.

3.3Gauss's Theorema Egregium

Since the plane is locally isometric to a cylinder, Example 3.3 and 3.4 show that the principal curvatures and the mean curvature are not isometric invariants. It is an astonishing fact that although neither \(\kappa_1\) and \(\kappa_2\) is invariant under isometrics, their product, the Gaussian curvature \(K\), is. This is the content of the Theorema Egregium of Gauss.

Another way to appreciate the significance of this theorem is to think in terms of isometric embeddings. We may think of portions of the plane and the cylinder as different isometric embeddings of the same plane region.

3.4The Gauss-Bonnet Theorem

For an oriented surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) the Gaussian curvature \(K\) is a function on the surface. If the surface is compact, we can integrate \(K\) to obtain a single number \(\int_M K d S\).

例 3.5. For the sphere \(M\) of radius \(a\), the integral of the Gaussian curvature is

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \int_M K d S & = \int_M rac{1}{a^2} d S = rac{1}{a^2} \int 1 d S\ & = rac{1}{a^2} (\operatorname{surface}\operatorname{area}\operatorname{of}M)\ & = rac{1}{a^2} 4 \pi a^2 = 4 \pi . \end{array}\)

Example 3.5 is a special case of the Gauss-Bonnet theorem, which for a compact oriented surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) asserts that

\(\displaystyle \int_M K d S = 2 \pi \chi (M),\) (3.2)

where \(\chi (M)\) denotes the Euler characteristic.

4Directional Derivatives in Euclidean Space

In this section, we extend the calculus definition of the directional derivative of a function to the directional derivative of a vector field along a submanifold of \(\mathbb{R}^n\).

4.1Directional Derivatives in Euclidean Space

Suppose \(X_p = \sum a^i \partial / \partial x^i |_p\) is a tangent vector at a point \(p = (p^1, \ldots, p^n)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and \(f (x^1, \ldots, x^n)\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) function in a neighborhood of \(p\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). To compute the directional derivative of \(f\) at \(p\) in the direction \(X_p\), we first write down a set of parametric equations for the line through \(p\) in the direction \(X_p\)

\(\displaystyle x^i = p^i + t a^i, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n.\)

Let \(a = (a^1, \ldots, a^n)\). Then the directional derivative \(D_{X_p} f\) is

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} D_{X_p} f & = \lim_{t ightarrow 0} rac{f (p + t a) - f (p)}{t} = rac{d}{d t} |_{t = 0} f (p + t a) \hspace{3cm} ext{(4.1)}\ & = \sum rac{\partial f}{\partial x^i} |_p \cdot rac{d x^i}{d t} |_{t = 0} = \sum rac{\partial f}{\partial x^i} |_p \cdot a^i\ & = \left( \sum a^i rac{\partial}{\partial x^i} |_p ight) f = X_p f. \end{array}\)

We write \(\partial_i\) for \(\partial / \partial x^i\). The directional derivative at \(p\) of a \(C^{\infty}\) vector field \(Y = \sum b^i \partial i = \sum b^i \partial / \partial x^i\) on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) in the direction \(X_p\) is defined to be

\(\displaystyle D_{X_p} Y = \sum (X_p b^i) rac{\partial}{\partial x^i} |_p .\) (4.2)

The formula shows that \(D_{X_p} Y\) is \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear in \(X_p\).

We can in fact use any curve \(c (t)\) with initial point \(p\) and initial vector \(X_p\), for

\(\displaystyle D_{X_p} f = X_p f = c' (0) f = c_{\ast} \left( rac{d}{d t} |_{t = 0} ight) f = rac{d}{d t} |_{t = 0} f (c (t)) .\)

注意 4.1. Thus, for \(D_{X_p} f\) to be defined, it is not necessary that \(f\) be defined in an open neighborhood of \(p\). As long as \(f\) is defined along some curve starting at \(p\) with initial velocity \(X_p\), the directional derivative will make sense. A similar remark applies to the directional derivative \(D_{X_p} Y\) of a vector field \(Y\).

When \(X\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) vector field on \(\mathbb{R}^n\), we define the vector field \(D_X Y\) on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) by

\(\displaystyle (D_X Y)_p = D_{X_p} Y \quad \operatorname{for}\operatorname{all}p \in \mathbb{R}^n .\)

Let \(\mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n)\) be the vector space of all \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields on \(\mathbb{R}^n\). The directional derivative in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) gives a map

\(\displaystyle D : \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n) imes \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n),\)

which we write as \(D_X Y\) instead of \(D (X, Y)\). Let \(\mathcal{F}= C^{\infty} (\mathbb{R}^n)\) be the ring of \(C^{\infty}\) functions on \(\mathbb{R}^n\). Then \(\mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n)\) is both a vector space over \(\mathbb{R}\) and a module over \(\mathcal{F}\).

命题 4.2. For \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n)\), the directional derivative \(D_X Y\) satisfies the following properties:

  1. \(D_X Y\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(X\) and \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear in \(Y\);

  2. (Leibniz rule) if \(f\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) function on \(\mathbb{R}^n\), then

    \(\displaystyle D_X (f Y) = (X f) Y + f D_X Y.\)

4.2Other Properties of the Directional Derivative

The directional derivative \(D\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is an \(\mathbb{R}\)-bilinear map

\(\displaystyle D : \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n) imes \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n),\)

one can ask if it is symmetric, that is, for all \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n)\), is \(D_X Y = D_Y X\)? A simple calculation using the standard frame shows that the answer is no; in fact, \([X, Y]\) is the Lie bracket defined in (A.2), then

\(\displaystyle D_X Y - D_Y X = [X, Y] .\)

The quantity

\(\displaystyle T (X, Y) = D_X Y - D_Y X - [X, Y]\)

turns out to be fundamental in differential geometry and is called the torsion of the directional derivative \(D\).

For each smooth vector field \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n)\), the directional derivative \(D_X : \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n)\) is an \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear endomorphism. This gives rise to a map

\(\mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n)\)

\( ightarrow \operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{R}} (\mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n))\)

(4.3)

\(X\)

\(\mapsto D_X\)

The vector space \(\mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n)\) of \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is a Lie algebra under the Lie bracket of vector fields. For any vector space \(V\), the endomorphism ring \(\operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{R}} (V)\) of endomorphisms of \(V\) is also a Lie algebra, with Lie bracket

\(\displaystyle [A, B] = A \circ B - B \circ A, \quad A, B \in \operatorname{End} (V) .\)

So the map in (4.3) is an \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear map of Lie algebras. It is natural to ask if its is a Lie algebra homomorphism, i.e., is

\(\displaystyle [D_X, D_Y] = D_{[X, Y]} ?\)

The answer is yes for the directional derivative in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). A measure of the deviation of the linear map \(X \mapsto D_X\) from being a Lie algebra homomorphism is given by the function

\(\displaystyle R (X, Y) = [D_X, D_Y] - D_{[X, Y]} = D_X D_Y - D_Y D_X - D_{[X, Y]} \in \operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{R}} (\mathfrak{X} (M)),\)

called the curvature of \(D\).

Finally, one may ask if the product rules holds for the Euclidean inner product:

\(\displaystyle D_Z \langle X, Y angle = \langle D_Z X, Y angle + \langle X, D_Z Y angle .\)

The answer is again yes.

命题 4.3. Let \(D\) be the directional derivative in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and \(X, Y, Z\) \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields on \(\mathbb{R}^n\). Then

  1. (zero torsion) \(D_X Y - D_Y X - [X, Y] = 0\),

  2. (zero curvature) \(D_X D_Y Z - D_Y D_X Z - D_{[X, Y]} Z = 0\),

  3. (compatibility with the metric) \(X \langle Y, Z angle = \langle D_X Y, Z angle + \langle Y, D_X Z angle\).

If \(X\) and \(Y\) are smooth vector fields on a manifold \(M\), then the Lie derivative \(\mathcal{L}_X Y\) is another way of differentiating \(Y\) with respect to \(X\) (for a discussion of the Lie derivative, see ). While the directional derivative \(D_X Y\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(X\), the Lie derivative \(\mathcal{L}_X Y\) is not, so the two concepts are not the same. Indeed, since \(\mathcal{L}_X Y = [X, Y]\), by Proposition 4.3(i), for vector fields on \(\mathbb{R}^n\),

\(\displaystyle \mathcal{L}_X Y = D_X Y - D_Y X.\)

4.3Vector Fields Along a Curve

定义 4.4. A vector field \(V\) along \(c : [a, b] ightarrow M\) is the assignment of a tangent vector \(V (t) \in T_{c (t)} M\). Such a vector field is said to be \(C^{\infty}\) if for every \(C^{\infty}\) function \(f\) on \(M\), the function \(V (t) f\) is \(C^{\infty}\) as a function of \(f\).

例 4.5. The velocity vector field \(c' (t)\) of a parametrized curve \(c\) is defined by

\(\displaystyle c' (t) = c_{\ast, t} \left( rac{d}{d t} |_t ight) \in T_{c (t)} M.\)

It is a vector field along \(c\).

例 4.6. If \( ilde{V}\) is a vector field on \(M\) and \(c : [a, b] ightarrow M\) is a parametrized curve in \(M\), then \( ilde{V}\) induces a vector field \(V\) along \(c\) by

\(\displaystyle V (t) = ilde{V}_{c (t)} .\)

\(V (t)\) can be written as a linear combination of the standard basis vectors:

\(\displaystyle V (t) = \sum v^i (t) \partial_i |_{c (t)} .\)

So it makes sense to differentiate \(V\) with respect to \(t\):

\(\displaystyle rac{d V}{d t} (t) = \sum rac{d v^i}{d t} (t) \partial_i |_{c (t)},\)

which is also a \(C^{\infty}\) vector field along \(c\).

For a smooth vector field \(V\) along a curve \(c\) in an arbitrary manifold \(M\), without further hypotheses on \(M\), the derivative \(d V / d t\) is in general not defined. This is becausse an arbitrary manifold does not have a canonical frame of vector fields such as \(\partial / \partial x^i, \ldots, \partial / \partial x^n\) on \(\mathbb{R}^n\).

命题 4.7. Let \(c : [a, b] ightarrow \mathbb{R}^n\) be a curve in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and let \(V (t), W (t)\) be smooth vector fields along \(c\). Then

\(\displaystyle rac{d}{d t} \langle V (t), W (t) angle = \left\langle rac{d V}{d t}, W ight angle + \left\langle V, rac{d W}{d t} ight angle .\)

4.4Vector Fields Along a Submanifold

定义 4.8. Let \(M\) be a submanifold of a manifold \( ilde{M}\). A vector field \(X\) on \(M\) assigns to each \(p \in M\) a tangent vector \(X_p \in T_p M\). A vector field along \(M\) in \( ilde{M}\) assigns to each \(p \in M\) a tangent vector \(X_p \in T_p ilde{M}\). A vector field \(X\) along \(M\) is called \(C^{\infty}\) if for every \(C^{\infty}\) function \(f\) on \( ilde{M}\), the function \(X f\) is \(C^{\infty}\) on \(M\).

The set of all \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields on a manifold \(M\) is denoted by \(\mathfrak{X} (M)\). The set of all \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields along a submanifold \(M\) in a manifold \( ilde{M}\) will be denoted by \(\Gamma (T ilde{M} |_M)\). They are both modules over the ring \(\mathcal{F}= C^{\infty} (M)\) of \(C^{\infty}\) functions on \(M\).

4.5Directional Derivatives on a Submanifold of \(\mathbb{R}^n\)

Suppose \(M\) is a regular submanifold of \(\mathbb{R}^n\).

For any \(p \in M\), we have \((D_X Y)_p = D_{X_p} Y\) by definition. it follows that there is an \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear map

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} D : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M) & ightarrow \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M)\ D (X, Y) & = D_X Y. \end{array}\)

We call \(D\) the directional derivative on \(M\).

命题 4.9. Suppose \(M\) is a regular submanifold of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and \(D\) is the directional derivative on \(M\). For \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(Y \in \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M)\),

  1. \(D (X, Y) = D_X Y\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(X\) and \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear in \(Y\);

  2. (Leibniz rule) if \(f \in C^{\infty} (M)\), then

    \(\displaystyle D (X, f Y) = D_X (f Y) = (X f) Y + f D_X Y.\)

命题 4.10. Suppose \(M\) is a regular submaniold of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and \(D\) is the directional derivative on \(M\).

  1. (zero curvature) If \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(Z \in \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M)\), then

    \(\displaystyle D_X D_Y Z - D_Y D_X Z - D_{[X, Y]} Z = 0.\)

  2. (compatibility with the metric) If \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(Y, Z \in \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M)\), then

    \(\displaystyle X \langle Y, Z angle = \langle D_X Y, Z angle + \langle Y, D_X Z angle .\)

命题 4.11. Differentiation with respect to \(t\) of a vector field along a curve is the directional derivative in the tangent direction:

\(\displaystyle rac{d V}{d t} = D_{c' (t)} ilde{V} .\)

5The Shape Operator

5.1Normal Vector Fields

命题 5.1. If 0 is a regular value of the \(C^{\infty}\) function \(f\) on \(\mathbb{R}^3\), then \(N =\operatorname{grad}f\) is a nowhere-vanishing normal vector field along the smooth hypersurface \(M = Z (f) .\)

5.2The Shape Operator

Let \(p\) be a point on a surface \(m\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) and let \(N\) be a \(C^{\infty}\) unit normal vector field on \(M\). For any tangent vector \(X_p \in T_p M\), define

\(\displaystyle L_p (X_p) = - D_{X_p} N\)

\(L_p\) is a linear map \(T_p M ightarrow T_p M\). It is called the shape operator or the Weingarten map of the surface \(M\) at \(p\).

引理 5.2. Let \(M\) be a surface in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) having a \(C^{\infty}\) unit normal vector field \(N\). For \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\),

\(\displaystyle \langle L (X), Y angle = \langle D_X Y, N angle .\)

证明. Differentiating the equation \(\langle Y, N angle \equiv 0\) with respect to \(X\).\(\Box\)

In general, if \(e_1, \ldots, e_n\) is a basis for a vector space \(V\) and

\(\displaystyle v = \sum_i v^i e_i \in V, \operatorname{with}v^i \in \mathbb{R},\) (5.1)

then \(v^i\) is called the component of \(v\) in the \(e_i\)-direction.

命题 5.3. The shape operator is self-adjoint: for any \(X_p, Y_p \in T_p M\),

\(\displaystyle \langle L (X_p), Y_p angle = \langle X_p, L (Y_p) angle .\)

证明.

\(\displaystyle \langle L (X), Y angle = \langle D_X Y, N angle\) (5.2)
\(\displaystyle \langle L (Y), X angle = \langle D_Y X, N angle .\) (5.3)
\(\displaystyle D_X Y - D_Y X = [X, Y] .\) (5.4)

Combining the three equaitons, we get

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} & \langle L (X), Y angle - \langle L (Y), X angle\ = & \langle D_X Y, N angle - \langle D_Y X, N angle\ = & \langle D_X Y - D_Y X, N angle\ = & \langle [X, Y], N angle\ = & 0 \quad (\operatorname{since} [X, Y] \operatorname{is}a\operatorname{tangent}\operatorname{vector}\operatorname{field}\operatorname{on}M) \end{array}\)

Hence,

\(\displaystyle \langle L (X), Y angle = \langle L (Y), X angle = \langle X, L (Y) angle .\)
\(\Box\)

If \(T : V ightarrow W\) is a linear map between two vector spaces \(V\) and \(W\), \(\mathcal{B}_V = \{ v_1, \ldots, v_n \}\) a basis for \(V\), and \(\mathcal{B}_W = \{ w_1, \ldots, w_m \}\) for \(W\), then

\(\displaystyle T (v_j) = \sum_{i = 1}^m {a ^i}_j w_i, \quad j = 1, \ldots, n,\)

for a unique matrix \([a^i_j]\), called the matrix of the linear map \(T\) with respect to the bases \(\mathcal{B}_V\) and \(\mathcal{B}_W\).

It follows from Proposition 5.3 that the matrix of the shape operator with respect to an orthonormal basis for \(T_p M\) is symmetric, for if \(e_1, e_2\) is an orthonormal basis for \(T_p M\) and

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} L (e_1) & = a e_1 + b e_c,\ L (e_2) & = h e_1 + c e_2, \end{array}\)

then

\(\displaystyle b = \langle L (e_1), e_2 angle = \langle e_1, L (e_2) angle = h.\)

Since the eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix are real, the shape operator has real eigenvalues. We will see the meaning of these eigenvalues.

5.3Curvature and the Shape Operator

Consider as before a surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) having a \(C^{\infty}\) unit normal vector field \(N\). Lemma 5.2 on the shape operator has a counterpart for vector fields along a curve.

命题 5.4. Let \(c : [a, b] ightarrow M\) be a curve in \(M\) and let \(V\) be a vector field in \(M\) along \(c\). Then

\(\displaystyle \langle L (c' (t)), V angle = \left\langle rac{d V}{d t}, N_{c (t)} ight angle .\)

注意. When we write \(V\) or \(d V / d t\), we mean \(V (t)\) and \(d V (t) / d t\), respectively.

证明. \(V (t)\) is tangent to \(M\), the inner product \(\langle V (t), N_{c (t)} angle\) is zero. Differentiating with respect to \(t\) yields

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} 0 & = rac{d}{d t} \langle V (t), N_{c (t)} angle\ & = \left\langle rac{d V}{d t}, N_{c (t)} ight angle + \left\langle V (t), rac{d}{d t} N_{c (t)} ight angle\ & = \left\langle rac{d V}{d t}, N_{c (t)} ight angle + \langle V (t), D_{c' (t)} N angle \end{array}\)

Thus,

\(\displaystyle \langle V (t), L (c' (t)) angle = \langle V (t), - D_{c' (t)} N angle = \left\langle rac{d V}{d t}, N_{c (t)} ight angle .\)
\(\Box\)

命题 5.5. Suppose \(\gamma (t)\) is a normal section, parametrized by arc length, determined by a unit tangent vector \(X_p \in T_p M\) and the unit normal vector \(N_p\). Then the normal curvature of \(\gamma (s)\) with respect to \(N_p\) at \(p\) is given by the second fundamental form:

\(\displaystyle \kappa (X_p) = \langle L (X_p), X_p angle = extrm{ extrm{ extrm{\mathrm{I} \mathrm{I}}}} (X_p, X_p)\)

证明. By definition, \(\gamma (0) = p\) and \(\gamma' (0) = X_p\). Let \(T (s) := \gamma' (s)\) be the unit tangent vector field along \(\gamma (s)\). Then the curvature of the normal section \(\gamma (s)\) is

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \kappa (\gamma' (s)) & = \langle \gamma'' (s), N_{\gamma (s)} angle\ & = \langle d T / d s, N_{\gamma (s)} angle\ & = \langle L (\gamma' (s)), T angle \quad (\operatorname{by}\operatorname{Proposition}5.4)\ & = \langle L (T), T angle . \end{array}\)

Evaluating at \(s = 0\) gives

\(\displaystyle \kappa (X_p) = \langle L (X_p), X_p angle = extrm{ extrm{\mathrm{I} \mathrm{I}} \langle X_p, X_p angle .}\)
\(\Box\)

命题 5.6. The principal directions of the surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) at \(p\) are the unit eigenvectors of the shape operator \(L\); the principal curvatures are the eigenvalues of \(L\).

证明. The principal curvatures at \(p\) are the maximum and minimum values of the function

\(\displaystyle \kappa (X_p) = extrm{\mathrm{I} \mathrm{I}} (X_p, X_p) = \langle L (X_p), X_p angle\)

for \(X_p \in T_p M\) satisfying \(\langle X_p, X_p angle = 1\). This type of optimization problem with a constraint lends itself to the method of the Lagrange multiplier from vector calculus.

Choose an orthonormal basis \(e_1, e_2\) for \(T_p M\) so that

\(\displaystyle X_p = x e_1 + y e_2 = \left[ egin{array}{c} x\ y \end{array} ight] .\)

By Proposition 5.3, the matrix of \(L\) relative to the basis \(e_1, e_2\) is a symmetric matrix

\(\displaystyle A = \left[ egin{array}{cc} a & b\ b & c \end{array} ight],\)

meaning

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} L (e_1) & = a e_1 + b e_c\ L (e_2) & = b e_1 + c e_2 \end{array}\)

In matrix notation,

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} L (X_P) & = L (x e_1 + y e_2) = (a x + b y) e_1 + (b x + c y) e_2\ & = \left[ egin{array}{c} a x + b y\ b x + c y \end{array} ight] = \left[ egin{array}{cc} a & b\ b & c \end{array} ight] \left[ egin{array}{c} x\ y \end{array} ight] = A X_p, \end{array}\)

and

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \kappa (X_p) & = \langle L (X_p), X_p angle = \langle A X_p, X_p angle = X_p^T A X_p\ & = a x^2 + 2 b x y + c y^2 . \end{array}\)

The problem of finding the principal curvatures becomes a standard calculus problem: find the maximum and minimum of the function

\(\displaystyle \kappa (X_p) = a x^2 + 2 b x y + c y^2 = X_p^T A X_p\)

subject to the constraint

\(\displaystyle g (X_p) = \langle X_p, X_p angle = x^2 + y^2 = 1.\)

Now

\(\displaystyle \operatorname{grad} \kappa = \left[ egin{array}{c} 2 a x + 2 b y\ 2 b x + 2 c y \end{array} ight] = 2 A X_p\)

and
