\(\displaystyle \operatorname{grad}g = 2 X_p .\)

By the method of Lagrange multiplier, at the maximum or minimum of \(\kappa\), there is a scalar \(\lambda\) such that

\(\displaystyle \operatorname{grad} \kappa = \lambda \operatorname{grad}g, \quad \operatorname{or}A X_p = \lambda X_p .\)

Thus, the maximum and minimum of \(\kappa\) occur at unit eigenvecrtors of \(A\). These are the principal directions at \(p\).

Let \(X_p\) be a principal direction at \(p\). Then the corresponding principal curvature is the normal curvature

\(\displaystyle \kappa (X_p) = \langle L (X_p), X_p angle = \langle A X_p, X_p angle = \langle \lambda X_p, X_p angle = \lambda \langle X_p, X_p angle = \lambda,\)

the eigenvalue associated to the eigenvector \(X_p\).\(\Box\)

推论 5.7.

  1. The Gaussian curvature of a surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) is the determinant of the shape operator.

  2. If \(e_1, e_2\) is an orthonormal basis for the tangent space \(T_p M\) of the surface \(M\), then the Gaussian curvature at \(p\) is

    \(\displaystyle K = \langle L (e_1), e_1 angle \langle L (e_2), e_2 angle - \langle L (e_1), e_2 angle \langle L (e_2), e_1 angle .\)

5.4The First and Second Fundamental Forms

A point \(p\) of a smooth surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), the Euclidean Riemannian metric is a symmetric bilinear form on the tangent space \(T_p M\). It is called the first fundamental form of \(M\) at \(p\). If \(L : T_p M ightarrow T_p M\) is the shape operator, the symmetric bilinear form on \(T_p M\)

\(\displaystyle \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (X_p, X_p) = \langle L (X_p), X_p angle\)

is called the second fundamental form of the surface of \(M\) at \(p\). The first fundamental form is the metric and the second fundamental form is essentially the shape operator. These two fundamental forms encode in them much of the geometry of the surface \(M\).

Let \(e_1, e_2\) be a basis for the tangent space \(T_p M\). We set

\(\displaystyle E := \langle e_1, e_1 angle, \quad F := \langle e_1, e_2 angle, \quad G := \langle e_2, e_2 angle .\)

If \(X_p = x^1 e_1 + x^2 e_2\) and \(Y = y^1 e_1 + y^2 e_2\), then

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \langle X_p, Y_p angle & = \langle e_1, e_1 angle x^1 y^1 + \langle e_1, e_2 angle x^1 y^2\ & \quad + \langle e_2, e_1 angle x^2 y^1 + \langle e_2, e_2 angle x^2 y^2\ & = E x^1 y^1 + F x^1 y^2 + F x^2 y^1 + G x^2 y^2\ & = \left[ egin{array}{cc} x^1 & x^2 \end{array} ight] \left[ egin{array}{cc} E & F\ F & G \end{array} ight] \left[ egin{array}{c} y^1\ y^2 \end{array} ight] . \end{array}\)

\(E, F, G\) completely determine the first fundamental form of \(M\) at \(p\). They are called the coefficients of the first fundamental form relative to \(e_1, e_2\).

Similarly, the three numbers

\(\displaystyle e := \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (e_1, e_2), \quad f := \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (e_1, e_2), \quad g := \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (e_2, e_2)\)

determine completely the second fundamental form of \(M\) at \(p\):

\(\displaystyle \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (X_p, Y_p) = \left[ egin{array}{cc} x^1 & x^2 \end{array} ight] \left[ egin{array}{cc} e & f\ f & g \end{array} ight] \left[ egin{array}{c} y^1 \ y^2 \end{array} ight] .\)

They are called the coefficients of the second fundamental form relative to \(e_1, e_2\). As \(p\) varies in an open set \(U\), if \(e_1, e_2\) remain a basis of \(T_p M\) at each point, these coefficients are six functions on \(U\). Classically, \(M\) is taken to be a coordinate patch \((U, u, v)\) with \(e_1 = \partial / \partial u\) and \(e_2 = \partial / \partial v\), and the differential geometry of \(M\) is done in terms of the six functions \(E, F, G, e, f, g\).

定理 5.8. Suppose \(M\) and \(M'\) are two smooth Riemannian manifolds of dimension 2, and \(\varphi : M ightarrow M'\) is a diffeomorphism. Let \(E, F, G\) be the coefficients of the first fundamental form relative to a frame \(e_1, e_2\) on \(M\), and \(E', F', G'\) be corresponding coefficients relative to \(e'_1 := \varphi_{\ast} e_1, e'_2 := \varphi_{\ast} e_2\) on \(M'\). Then \(\varphi\) is an isometry if and only if E,F,G at \(p\) are equal to \(E', F', G'\) at \(\varphi (p)\)„ respectively, for all \(p \in M\).

5.5The Catenoid and the Helicoid

The graph of the hyperbolic cosine function

\(\displaystyle y = \cosh x := rac{e^x + e^{- x}}{2}\)

is called a catenary.

The surface of revolution obtained by rotating the catenary \(r = \cosh z\) about the \(z\)-axis is called a catenoid. It has parameterization

\(\displaystyle (r \cos heta, r \sin heta, z) = ((\cosh u) \cos heta, (\cosh u) \sin heta, u),\)

where we set \(z = u\)

The helicoid is the surface with parameterization

\(\displaystyle (r \cos heta, r \sin heta, heta), \quad - \infty < r, heta < \infty ;\)

it is the surface traced out by a horizontal stick moving upward with constant speed while rotating about a vertical axis through its midpoint.

As coordinate charts, there are diffeomorphisms \(U \simeq (- 1, 1) imes (0, 2 \pi)\) and \(U' \simeq (- a, a) imes (0, 2 \pi)\). Since \((- 1, 1)\) is diffeomorphic to \((- \sinh 1, \sinh 1)\) via the map \(u \mapsto \sinh u\), there is a diffeomorphism \(\varphi : U ightarrow U'\) given by

\(\displaystyle ((\cosh u) \cos heta, (\cosh u) \sin heta, u) \mapsto ((\sinh u) \cos heta, (\sinh u) \sin heta, heta) .\)

By computing the three functions \(E, F, G\) relative to the frame \(e*1 = \partial / \partial u, e_2 = \partial / \partial heta\) on the catenoid and the frame \(e'\_1 = \varphi*{\ast} e*1, e'\_2 = \varphi*{\ast} e*2\) on the helicoid, respectively, one can show that \(\varphi : U ightarrow U'\) is an isometry. Physically, what this means is that if one cuts the catenoid along a meridian, straighten out the meridian, and let the catenoid hang, it will assume the shape of the helicoid.

6Affine Connections

Consider a smooth vector field \(Y\) on a manifold \(M\) and a tangent vector \(X_p \in T_p M\) at a point \(p\) in \(M\). To define the directional derivative of \(Y\) in the direction \(X_p\), it is necessary to compare the values of \(Y\) in a neighborhood of \(p\). If \(q\) is a point near \(p\), in general it is not possible to compare the vectors \(Y_q\) and \(Y_p\) , since they are in distinct tangent spaces. Instead, we extract from the directional derivative in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) certain key properties and call any map \(D : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \mathfrak{X} (M) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (M)\) with these properties an affine connection. Intuitively, an affine connection on a manifold is simply a way of differentiating vector fields on the manifold.

We will see later that there are infinitely many affine connections on any manifold. On a Riemannian manifold, there is a unique torsion-free affine connection compatible with the metric, called the Riemannian or Levi-Civita connection.

6.1Affine Connections

We can define the directional derivative of a \(C^{\infty}\) function in the direction \(X_p \in T_p M\) in the same way as before

\(\displaystyle abla*{X*p} f = X_p f.\)

However, there is no longer a canonical way to define the directional derivative of a vector field \(Y\). Formula (4.2) fails because unlike in \(\mathbb{R}^n\), there is no canonical basis for the tangent space \(T_p M\).

定义 6.1. An affine connection on a manifold \(M\) is an \(\mathbb{R}\)-bilinear map

\(\displaystyle abla : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \mathfrak{X} (M) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (M),\)

written \( abla_X Y\) for \( abla (X, Y)\), satisfying the two properties below: if \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ring \(C^{\infty} (M)\) of \(C^{\infty}\) functions on \(M\), then for all \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\),

  1. \( abla_X Y\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(X\),

  2. (Leibniz rule) \( abla_X Y\) satisfies the Leibniz rule in \(Y\): for \(f \in \mathcal{F}\),

    \(\displaystyle abla_X (f Y) = (X f) Y + f abla_X Y.\)

例 6.2. The directional derivative \(D_X\) of a vector field \(Y\) on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is an affine connection on \(\mathbb{R}^n\), sometimes called the Euclidean connection on \(\mathbb{R}^n\).

6.2Torsion and Curvature

Given an affine connection \( abla\) on a manifold \(M\), one might ask whether it satisfies the same properties as Proposition 4.3 for the Euclidean connection on \(\mathbb{R}^n\). For \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), set

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} T (X, Y) & = abla_X Y - abla_Y X - [X, Y] \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\ R (X, Y) & = [ abla_X, abla_Y] - abla*{[X, Y]}\ & = abla*X abla Y - abla_Y abla_X - abla*{[X, Y]} \in \operatorname{End} (\mathfrak{X} (M)) \end{array}\)

We call \(T\) the torsion and \(R\) the curvature of the connection.

An affine connection \( abla\) on a manifold \(M\) gives rise to a linear map

\(\displaystyle \mathfrak{X} (M) ightarrow \operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{R}} (\mathfrak{X} (M)), \quad X \mapsto abla_X .\)

Here both vector spaces \(\mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(\operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{R}} (\mathfrak{X} (M))\) are Lie algebras. The curvature means the deviation of the map \(X \mapsto abla*X\) from being a Lie algebra homomorphism.

Although \( abla_X Y\) is not \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(Y\), it turns out, amazingly, that both torsion and curvature are \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in all their arguments.

命题 6.3. Let \(X, Y, Z\) be smooth vector fields on a manifold \(M\) with affine connection \( abla\).

  1. The torsion \(T (X, Y)\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(X\) and in \(Y\);

  2. The curvature \(R (X, Y) Z\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in \(X, Y\) and \(Z\).

证明.

if \(f, g \in \mathcal{F}= C^{\infty} (M)\), then

\(\displaystyle [f X, g Y] = f g [X, Y] + f (X g) Y - g (Y f) X.\) (6.1)
\(\displaystyle R (f X, Y) Z = abla*{f X} abla*Y Z - abla_Y abla*{f X} Z - abla\_{[f X, Y]} Z.\) (6.2)

\(\Box\)

6.3The Riemannian Connection

We say that a connection is torsion-free if its torsion is zero. On a Riemannian manifold, we say that a connection is compatible with the metric if for all \(X, Y, Z \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\),

\(\displaystyle Z \langle X, Y angle = \langle abla_Z X, Y angle + \langle X, abla_Z Y angle .\)

定义 6.4. On a Riemannian manifold, a Riemannian connection, sometimes called a Levi-Civita connection, is an affine connection that is torsion-free and compatible with the metric.

引理 6.5. A \(C^{\infty}\) vector field \(X\) on a Riemannian manifold \((M, \langle, angle)\) is uniquely determined by the values \(\langle X, Z angle\) for all \(Z \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\).

定理 6.6. On a Riemannian manifold, there is a unique Riemannian connection.

证明.

By Lemma 6.5, to specify \( abla_X Y\), it suffices to know \(\langle abla_X Y, Z angle\) for every vector field \(Z \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\). So we will try to find a formula for \(\langle abla_X Y, Z angle\) involving only the Riemannian metric and canonical operations on vector fields such as the Lie bracket.

A Riemannian connection satisfies the two formulas

\(\displaystyle abla_X Y - abla_Y X - [X, Y] = 0\) (6.3)

and

\(\displaystyle X \langle Y, Z angle = \langle abla_X Y, Z angle + \langle Y, abla_X Z angle .\) (6.4)

Cyclically permuting \(X, Y, Z\) in (6.4) gives two other formulas:

\(\displaystyle Y \langle Z, X angle = \langle abla_Y Z, X angle + \langle Z, abla_Y X angle,\) (6.5)
\(\displaystyle Z \langle X, Y angle = \langle abla_Z X, Y angle + \langle X, abla_Z Y angle\) (6.6)

Using (6.3), we can rewrite \( abla_Y X\) in (6.5) in terms of \( abla_X Y\):

\(\displaystyle Y \langle Z, X angle = \langle abla_Y Z, X angle + \langle Z, abla_Y X angle - \langle Z, [X, Y] angle\) (6.7)

Subtracting (6.6) from (6.4) and then adding (6.7) to it will create terms involving \( abla_X Z - abla_Z X\) and \( abla_Y Z - abla_Z Y\), which are equal to \([X, Z]\) and \([Y, Z]\) by torsion-freeness:

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} X \langle Y, Z angle & + Y \langle Z, X angle - Z \langle X, Y angle\ & = 2 \langle abla_X Y, Z angle + \langle Y, abla_X Z - abla_Z X angle + \langle X, abla_Y Z - abla_Z Y angle - \langle Z, [X, Y] angle\ & = 2 \langle abla_X Y, Z angle + \langle Y, [X, Z] angle + \langle X, [Y, Z] angle - \langle Z, [X, Y] angle . \end{array}\)

Solving for \(\langle abla_X Y, Z angle\), we get

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} 2 \langle abla_X Y, Z angle = & X \langle Y, Z angle + Y \langle Z, X angle - Z \langle X, Y angle \hspace{3cm} ext{(6.8)}\ & - \langle X, [Y, Z] angle + \langle Y, [Z, X] angle + \langle Z, [X, Y] angle . \end{array}\)

This formula proves that a Riemannian connection, if it exists, is unique.

Define \( abla_X Y\) by (6.8). It is a straightforward exercise to check that so defined, \( abla\) is a torsion-free affine connection compactible with the metric. This proves the existence of a Riemannian connection on a Riemannian manifold.\(\Box\)

7Vector Bundles

The set \(\mathfrak{X} (M)\) of all \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields on a manifold has the structure of a real vector spacce, which is the same as a module over the field \(\mathbb{R}\) of real numbers. Let \(\mathcal{F}= C^{\infty} (M)\) again be the ring of \(C^{\infty}\) functions on \(M\). Since we can multiply a vector field by a \(C^{\infty}\) function, the vector space \(\mathfrak{X} (M)\) is also a module over \(\mathcal{F}\).

We will try to understand \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear maps from the point of view of vector bundles. The main result (Theorem 7.26) asserts the existence of a one-to-one correspondence between \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear maps \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) of sections of vector bundles and bundle maps \(\varphi : E ightarrow F\).

7.1Definition of a Vector Bundle

定义 7.1. A \(C^{\infty}\) surjection \(\pi : E ightarrow M\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundle of rank \(r\) if

  1. For every \(p \in M\), the set \(E_p := \pi^{- 1} (p)\) is a real vector space of dimension \(r\);

  2. every point \(p \in M\) has an open neighborhood \(U\) such that there is a fiber-preserving diffeomorphism

    \(\displaystyle \phi_U : \pi^{- 1} (U) ightarrow U imes \mathbb{R}^r\)

    that restricts to a linear isomorphism \(E_p ightarrow \{ p \} imes \mathbb{R}^r\) on each fiber.

The space \(E\) is called the total space, the space \(M\) the base space, and the space \(E_p\) the fiber above \(p\) of the vector bundle. We often say that \(E\) is a vector bundle over \(M\). A vector bundle of rank 1 is also called a line bundle.

例 7.2. (Product Bundle)

例 7.3. (Mobius strip).

例 7.4. (Restriction of a vector bundle).

定义 7.5. Let \(\pi_E : E ightarrow M\) and \(\pi_F : F ightarrow N\) be a \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundles. A \(C^{\infty}\) bundle map from \(E\) to \(F\) is a pair of \(C^{\infty}\) maps \(\left( \varphi : E ightarrow F, \underline{\varphi} : M ightarrow N ight)\) such that

  1. the diagram

    commutes.

  2. \(\varphi\) restricts to a linear map \(\varphi*p : E_p ightarrow F*{\underline{\varphi} (p)}\) of fibers for each \(p \in M\).

Abusing language, we often call the map \(\varphi : E ightarrow F\) along the bundle map.

An important special case of a bundle map occurs when \(E\) and \(F\) are vector fields over the same manifold \(M\) and the base map \(\underline{\varphi}\) is the identity map. In this case, we call the bundle map \((\varphi : E ightarrow F, 1*M)\) a bundle map over \(M\).

If there is a bundle map \(\psi : F ightarrow E\) over \(M\) such that \(\psi \circ \varphi = 1_E\) and \(\varphi \circ \psi = 1_F\), then \(\varphi\) is called a bundle isomorphism over \(M\), and the vector bundles \(E\) and \(F\) are said to be isomorphic over \(M\).

定义 7.6. A vector bundle \(\pi : E ightarrow M\) is said to be trivial if it is isomorphic to a product bundle \(M imes \mathbb{R}^r ightarrow M\) over \(M\).

例 7.7. (Tangent bundle) For any manifold \(M\), define \(T M\) to be the set of all tangent vectors of \(M\)

\(\displaystyle T M = \{ (p, v) |p \in M, v \in T_p M \} .\)

If \(U\) is a coordinate open subset of \(M\), then \(T U\) is bijective with the product bundle \(U imes \mathbb{R}^n\). We give \(T M\) the topology generated by \(T U\) as \(U\) runs over all coordinate open subsets of \(M\). In this way \(T M\) can be given a manifold structure so that \(T M ightarrow M\) becomes a vector bundle. It is called the tangent bundle of \(M\).

例 7.8. If \(f : M ightarrow N\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) map of manifolds, then its differential gives rise to a bundle map \(f*{\ast} : T M ightarrow T N\) defined by

\(\displaystyle f*{\ast} (p, v) = (f (p), f*{\ast, p} (v)) .\)

7.2The Vector Space of Sections

A section of a vector bundle \(\pi : E ightarrow M\) over an open set \(U\) is a function \(s : U ightarrow E\) such that \(\pi \circ s = 1*U\), the identity map on \(U\). For each \(p \in U\), the section \(s\) picks out one element of the fiber \(E_p\). The set of all \(C^{\infty}\) sections of \(E\) over \(U\) is denoted by \(\Gamma (U, E)\). If \(U\) is the manifold \(M\), we also write \(\Gamma (E)\) instead of \(\Gamma (M, E)\).

The set \(\Gamma (U, E)\) of \(C^{\infty}\) sections of \(E\) over \(U\) is clearly a vector space over \(\mathbb{R}\). It is in fact a module over the ring \(C^{\infty} (U)\) of \(C^{\infty}\) functions, for if \(f\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) function over \(U\) and \(s\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) section of \(E\) over \(U\), then the definition

\(\displaystyle (f s) (p) := f (p) s (p) \in E_p, \quad p \in U,\)

makes \(f s\) into a \(C^{\infty}\) section of \(E\) over \(U\).

例 7.9. (Sections of a product line bundle) A section \(s\) of the product bundle \(M imes \mathbb{R} ightarrow M\) is a map \(s (p) = (p, f (p))\). So there is a one-to-one correspondence

\(\displaystyle \{ \operatorname{section}\operatorname{of}M imes \mathbb{R} ightarrow M \} \longleftrightarrow \{ \operatorname{functions}f : M ightarrow \mathbb{R} \} .\)

In particular, the space of \(C^{\infty}\) sections of the product line bundle \(M imes \mathbb{R} ightarrow M\) may be identified with \(C^{\infty} (M)\).

例 7.10. (Sections of the tangent bundle). A vector field on a manifold \(M\) assigns to each point \(p \in M\) a tangent vector \(X_p \in T_p M\). Therefore, it is precisely a section of the tangent bundle \(T M\). Thus, \(\mathfrak{X} (M) = \Gamma (T M)\).

例 7.11. (Vector fields along a submanifold). If \(M\) is a regular submanifold of \(\mathbb{R}^n\), then a \(C^{\infty}\) vector field along \(M\) is precisely a section of the restriction \(T\mathbb{R}^n |\_M\) of \(T\mathbb{R}^n\) to \(M\). This explains our earlier notation \(\Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^3 |\_M)\) for the space of \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields along \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\).

定义 7.12. A bundle map \(\varphi : E ightarrow F\) over a manifold \(M\) (meaning that the base map is the identity \(1_M\)) induces a map on the sapce of sections:

\(\displaystyle \varphi*{\#} : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F),\)

\(\displaystyle \varphi*{\#} (s) = \varphi \circ s.\)

The induced map \(\varphi*{\#}\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear becuase if \(f \in \mathcal{F}\), then

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} (\varphi*{\#} (f s)) (p) & = (\varphi \circ (f s) (p)) = \varphi (f (p) s (p))\ & = f (p) \varphi (s (p))\ & = (f \varphi*{\#} (s)) (p) \end{array}\)

Our goal in the rest of this chapter is to prove that conversely, every \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear map \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) comes from a bundle map \(\varphi : E ightarrow F\), i.e., \(\alpha = \varphi*{\#}\).

7.3Extending a Local Section to a Global Section

The example of the tangent function \( an : (- \pi / 2, \pi / 2) ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) shows that it may not be possible to extend the domain of a \(C^{\infty}\) function \(f : U ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) from an open set \(U \subset M\) to the manifold \(M\). However, given a point \(p \in U\), it is always possible to find a \(C^{\infty}\) global function \( ilde{f} : M ightarrow \mathbb{R}\) that agrees with \(f\) on some neighborhood of \(p\). More generally, this is also true for sections of a vector bundle.

命题 7.13. Let \(E ightarrow M\) be a \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundle, \(s\) a \(C^{\infty}\) section of \(E\) over some open set \(U\) in \(M\), and \(p\) a point in \(U\). Then there exists a \(C^{\infty}\) global section \( ar{s} \in \Gamma (M, E)\) that agrees with \(s\) over some neighborhood of \(p\).

证明. Choose a \(C^{\infty}\) bump function \(f\) on \(M\) such that \(f \equiv 1\) on a neighborhood \(W\) of \(p\) contained in \(U\) and \(\operatorname{supp}f \subset U\). Define \( ar{s} : M ightarrow E\) by

\(\displaystyle ar{s} = \left\{ egin{array}{ll} f (q) s (q) & \operatorname{for}q \in U,\ 0 & \operatorname{for}q ot\in U. \end{array} ight.\)

\(\Box\)

7.4Local Operators

定义 7.14. Let \(E\) and \(F\) be vector bundles over a manifold \(M\). An \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear map \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) is a local operator if whenever a section \(s \in \Gamma (E)\) vanishes on an open set \(U\) in \(M\), then \(\alpha (s) \in \Gamma (F)\) also vanishes on \(U\). It is a point operator if whenever a section \(s \in \Gamma (E)\) vanishes at a point \(p\) in \(M\), then \(\alpha (s) \in \Gamma (F)\) also vanishes at \(p\).

例 7.15. By Example 7.9, the vector space \(C^{\infty} (\mathbb{R})\) of \(C^{\infty}\) functions on \(\mathbb{R}\) may be identified with the vector space \(\Gamma (\mathbb{R} imes \mathbb{R})\) of \(C^{\infty}\) sections of the product line bundle over \(\mathbb{R}\). The derivative

\(\displaystyle rac{d}{d t} : C^{\infty} (\mathbb{R}) ightarrow C^{\infty} (\mathbb{R})\)

is a local operator since if \(f (t) \equiv 0\) on \(U\), then \(f' (t) \equiv 0\) on \(U\). However, \(d / d t\) is not a point operator.

例 7.16. Let \(\Omega^k (M)\) denote the vector space of \(C^{\infty}\) \(k\)-forms on a manifold \(M\). Then the exterior derivative \(d : \Omega^k (M) ightarrow \Omega^{k + 1} (M)\) is a local operator.

命题 7.17. Let \(E\) and \(F\) be \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundles over a manifold \(M\), and \(\mathcal{F}= C^{\infty} (M)\). If a map \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear, then it is a local operator.

例 7.18. On a \(C^{\infty}\) manifold \(M\), a derivation \(D : C^{\infty} (M) ightarrow C^{\infty} (M)\) is \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear, but not \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear since by the Leibniz rule,

\(\displaystyle D (f g) = (D f) g + f D g, \quad \operatorname{for}f, g \in \mathcal{F}.\)

However, by Problem 7.1, \(D\) is a local operator.

例 7.19. Fix a \(C^{\infty}\) vector field \(X \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\). Then a connection \( abla\) on \(M\) induces a map

\(\displaystyle abla_X : \mathfrak{X} (M) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (M)\)

that satisfies the Leibniz rule. By problem 7.2, \( abla_X\) is a local operator.

7.5Restriction of a Local Operator to an Open Subset

A continuous global section of a vector bundle can always be restricted to an open subset, but in general a section over an open subset cannnot be extended to a continuous global section. A local operator, which is defined on global sections of a vector bundle, can always be restricted to an open subset.

定理 7.20. Let \(E\) and \(F\) be vector bundle over a manifold \(M\). If \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) is a local operator, then for each open subset \(U\) of \(M\) there is a unique linear map, called the restriction of \(\alpha\) to \(U\),

\(\displaystyle \alpha_U : \Gamma (U, E) ightarrow \Gamma (U, F)\)

such that for any global section \(t \in \Gamma (E)\),

\(\displaystyle \alpha_U (t |\_U) = \alpha (t) |\_U .\) (7.1)

命题 7.21. Let \(E\) and \(F\) be \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundles over a manifold \(M\), let \(U\) be an open subset of \(M\), and let \(\mathcal{F} (U) = C^{\infty} (U)\), the ring of \(C^{\infty}\) functions on \(U\). If \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear, then the restriction \(\alpha_U : \Gamma (U, E) ightarrow \Gamma (U, F)\) is \(\mathcal{F} (U)\)-linear.

7.6Frames

A frame for a vector bundle \(E\) of rank \(r\) over an open subset \(U\) is a collection of sections \(e_1, \ldots, e_r\) of \(E\) over \(U\) such that at each point \(p \in U\), the elements \(e_1 (p), \ldots, e_r (p)\) form a basis for the fiber \(E_p\).

命题 7.22. A \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundle \(\pi : E ightarrow M\) is trivial if and only if it has a \(C^{\infty}\) frame.

It follows from this proposition that over any trivializing open set \(U\) of a vector bundle \(E\), there is always a frame.

7.7\(\mathcal{F}\)-Linearity and Bundle Maps

Throughout this subsection, \(E\) and \(F\) are \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundles over a manifold \(M\), and \(\mathcal{F}= C^{\infty} (M)\) is the ring of \(C^{\infty}\) real-valued functions on \(M\). We will show that an \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear map \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) can be defined pointwise and therefore corresponds uniquely to a bundle map \(E ightarrow F\).

引理 7.23. An \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear map \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) is a point operator.

引理 7.24. Let \(E\) and \(F\) be \(C^{\infty}\) vector bundles over a manifold \(M\). A fiber-preserving map \(\varphi : E ightarrow F\) that is linear on each fiber is \(C^{\infty}\) if and only if \(\varphi*{\#}\) takes \(C^{\infty}\) sections of \(E\) to \(C^{\infty}\) sections of \(F\).

命题 7.25. If \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear, then for each \(p \in M\), there is a unique linear map \(\varphi*p : E_p ightarrow F_p\) such that for all \(s \in \Gamma (E)\),

\(\displaystyle \varphi_p (s (p)) = \alpha (s) (p) .\)

定理 7.26. There is a one-to-one correspondence

\(\displaystyle \{ \operatorname{bundle}\operatorname{maps} \varphi : E ightarrow F \} \longleftrightarrow \{ \mathcal{F} ext{-linear maps} \alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F) \}\)

\(\displaystyle \varphi \mapsto \varphi*{\#} .\)

推论 7.27. An \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear map \(\omega : \mathfrak{X} (M) ightarrow C^{\infty} (M)\) is a \(C^{\infty}\) 1-form on \(M\).

7.8Multilinear Maps over Smooth Functions

By Proposition 7.25, if \(\alpha : \Gamma (E) ightarrow \Gamma (F)\) is an \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear map of sections of vector bundles over \(M\), then at each \(p \in M\), it si possible to define a linear map \(\varphi*p : E_p ightarrow F_p\) such that for any \(s \in \Gamma (E)\),

\(\displaystyle \varphi_p (s (p)) = \alpha (s) (p) .\)

This can be generalized to \(\mathcal{F}\)-multilinear maps.

命题 7.28. Let \(E, E', F\) be vector bundles over a manifold \(M\). If

\(\displaystyle \alpha : \Gamma (E) imes \Gamma (E') ightarrow \Gamma (F)\)

is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear, then for each \(p \in M\) there is a unique \(\mathbb{R}\)-bilinear map

\(\displaystyle \varphi_p : E_p imes E'\_p ightarrow F_p\)

such that for all \(s \in \Gamma (E)\) and \(s' \in \Gamma (E')\),

\(\displaystyle \varphi_p (s (p), s' (p)) = (\alpha (s, s')) (p) .\)

Proposition 7.28 generalizes to \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear maps with \(k\) arguments. Just as in Corollary 7.27, we conclude that is an alternating map

\(\displaystyle \omega : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \ldots imes \mathfrak{X} (M) (k\operatorname{times}) ightarrow C^{\infty} (M)\)

is \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear in each argument, then \(\omega\) induces a \(k\)-form \( ilde{\omega}\) on \(M\) such that for \(X_1, \ldots, X_k \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\),

\(\displaystyle ilde{\omega} (X*{1, p}, \ldots, X_k, p) = (\omega (X_1, \ldots, X_k)) (p) .\)

It is customary to write the \(k\)-form \( ilde{\omega}\) as \(\omega\).

8Gauss's Theorema Egregium

8.1The Gauss and Codazzi-Mainardi Equations

defined in Section 4.5 the directional derivative \(D_X Z\). It is a vector field along \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\).

For \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) and \(Z \in \Gamma (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M)\), we verified in Proposition 4.10 the equation

\(\displaystyle R (X, Y) Z := D_X D_Y Z - D_Y D_X Z - D_{[X, Y]} Z = 0.\) (8.1)

Note that this is not the same curvature operator as the curvature operator of the directional derivative on \(\mathbb{R}^n\). The earlier curvature operator was a map

\(\displaystyle R : \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n) imes \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n) imes \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (\mathbb{R}^n) ;\)

the current curvature operator is a map

\(\displaystyle R : \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \mathfrak{X} (M) imes \mathfrak{X} (T\mathbb{R}^n |_M) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (T\mathbb{R}^n |\_M) .\)

Since the Riemannian connection \( abla\) of a surface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) is defined in terms of the directional derivative \(D\) on \(M\), it is easy to compare the curvature tensors of \( abla\) and \(D\). This will lead to a formula for the curvature \(R\) of \( abla\), called the Gauss curvature equation.

A vector field \(Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) is also a vector field along \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\). Hence, if \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), the directional derivative \(D_X Y\) makes sense. Assume that \(M\) is oriented with a unit normal vector field \(N\). At any point \(p \in M\), the normal component of the vector \(D_{X*p} Y\) is \(\langle D*{X*p} Y, N_p angle N_p\), and therefore the tangent component is

\(\displaystyle \operatorname{pr} (D*{X*p} Y) = D*{X*p} Y - \langle D*{X*p} Y, N_p angle N_p .\)

By (6.9), this is the definition of the Riemannian connection \( abla*{X*p} Y\) on \(M\). Hence,

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} D*{X*p} Y & = abla*{X*p} Y + \langle D*{X*p} Y, N_p angle N_p\ & = abla*{X*p} Y + \langle L_p (X_p), Y_p angle N_p \hspace{3cm} ext{(8.2)} \end{array}\)

Gloablizing this equation, we have, for \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\),

\(\displaystyle D_X Y = abla_X Y + \langle L (X), Y angle N,\) (8.3)

which decomposes the vector field \(D_X Y\) into its tangential and normal components.

定理 8.1. Let \(M\) be an oriented surface in \(\mathbb{R}^3\), \( abla\) its Riemannian connection. \(R\) the curvature operator of \( abla\), and \(L\) the shape operator on \(M\). For \(X, Y, Z \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\),

  1. (Gauss curvature equation)

    \(\displaystyle R (X, Y) Z = \langle L (Y), Z angle L (X) - \langle L (X), Z angle L (Y) ;\)

  2. (Codazzi-Mainardi equation)

    \(\displaystyle abla_X L (Y) - abla_Y L (X) - L ([X, Y]) = 0.\)

证明.

  1. Decomposing \(D_Y Z\) into its tangential and normal components, one has

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} D_Y Z & = (D_Y Z)*{ an} + (D*Y Z)*{\operatorname{nor}}\ & = abla_Y Z + \langle L (Y), Z angle N. \hspace{3cm} ext{(8.4)} \end{array}\)

    Hence,

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} D_X D_Y Z & = D_X D_Y Z + D_X (\langle L (Y), Z angle N)\ & = abla_X abla_Y Z + \langle L (X), abla_Y Z angle N + X \langle L (Y), Z angle N + \langle L (Y), Z angle D_X N\ & (\operatorname{by} (8.4) \operatorname{and}\operatorname{Leibniz}\operatorname{rule})\ & = abla*X abla_Y Z - \langle L (X), Z angle L (X) + \langle L (Y), abla_X Z angle N + Y \langle L (X), Z angle N. \hspace{3cm} ext{(8.5)} \end{array}\)

    Interchanging \(X\) and \(Y\) gives

    \(\displaystyle D_Y D_X Z = abla_X abla_Y Z - \langle L (X), Z angle L (Y) + \langle L (Y), abla_X Z angle N + Y \langle L (X), Z angle N.\) (8.6)

    By (8.4)

    \(\displaystyle D*{[X, Y]} Z = abla\_{[X, Y]} Z + \langle L ([X, Y]), Z angle N.\) (8.7)

    Subtracting (8.6) and (8.7) from (8.5) gives

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} 0 = & R_D (X, Y) Z = R (X, Y) Z - \langle L (Y), Z angle L (X) + \langle L (X), Z angle L (Y)\ & +\operatorname{normal}\operatorname{component}. \hspace{3cm} ext{(8.8)} \end{array}\)

    The tangential component of (8.8) gives

    \(\displaystyle R (X, Y) = \langle L (Y), Z angle L (X) - \langle L (X), Z angle L (Y)\)

    which is the Gauss curvature equation.

  2. The normal component of (8.8) is

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} ( \langle L (X), abla_Y Z angle + X \langle L (Y), Z angle & \ - \langle L (Y), abla*X Z angle - Y \langle L (X), Z angle & - \langle L ([X, Y]), Z angle ) N = 0. \end{array}\)

    By the compatibility of \( abla\) with the metric, this simplies to

    \(\displaystyle \langle abla_X L (Y), Z angle - \langle abla_Y L (X), Z angle - \langle L ([X, Y]), Z angle = 0.\)

    Since the equation above is true for all \(Z\), by the nondegeneracy of the inner product,

    \(\displaystyle abla_X L (Y) - abla_Y L (X) - L ([X, Y]) = 0.\)

\(\Box\)

注意 8.2. By the generalization of Proposition 7.28 to \(\mathcal{F}\)-trilinear maps, because \(R (X, Y) Z\) is \(\mathcal{F}\)-trilinear in \(X, Y, Z\), for each \(p \in M\), there is a unique \(\mathbb{R}\)-trilinear map

\(\displaystyle R_p : T_p M imes T_p M imes T_p M ightarrow T_p M\)

such that \(R_p (X_p, Y_p) Z_p = (R (X, Y) Z)\_p\). Thus, although we have stated the Gauss curvature equation for vector fields, it is also true for vectors \(X_p, Y_p, Z_p \in T_p M\) at a point \(p\).

8.2A Proof of the Theorema Egregium

Suppose for every Riemannian manifold \(M\), there is defined a function \(f_M : M ightarrow \mathbb{R}\). The function \(f_M\) is said to be an isometric invariant if for every isometry \(\varphi : M ightarrow ilde{M}\) of Riemannian manifolds, we have \(f_M (p) = f*{ ilde{M}} (\varphi (p))\) for all \(p \in M\).

定理 8.3. (Theorema Egregium). Let \(M\) be a surface in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) and \(p\) a point in \(M\).

  1. If \(e_1, e_2\) is an orthonormal basis for the tangent plane \(T_p M\), then the Gaussian curvature \(K_p\) of \(M\) at \(p\) is

    \(\displaystyle K_p = \langle R_p (e_1, e_2) e_2, e_1 angle .\) (8.9)

  2. The Gaussian curvature is an isometric invariant of smooth surfaces in \(\mathbb{R}^3\).

证明.

  1. In Corollary 5.7, we found a formula for the Gaussian curvature \(K_p\) in terms of the shape operator \(L\) and an orthonormal basis \(e_1, e_2\) for \(T_p M\):

    \(\displaystyle K_p = \langle L (e_1), e_1 angle \langle L (e_2), e_2 angle - \langle L (e_1), e_2 angle \langle L (e_2), e_1 angle .\)

    By the Gauss curvature equation,

    \(\displaystyle R_p (e_1, e_2) e_2 = \langle L (e_2), e_2 angle L (e_1) - \langle L (e_1), e_2 angle L (e_2) .\)

    Take the inner product with \(e_1\) gives

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \langle R_p (e_1, e_2) e_2, e_1 angle & = \langle L (e_2), e_2 angle \langle L (e_1), e_1 angle - \langle L (e_1), e_2 angle \langle L (e_2), e_1 angle\ & = K_p . \end{array}\)

  2. Since \(R_p (e_1, e_2)\) is determined completely by the metric, by Formula (8.9) the same can be said for \(K_p\). A detailed proof is left to Problem 8.3.

\(\Box\)

8.3The Gaussian Curvature in Terms of an Arbitrary Basis

命题 8.4. Let \(M\) be a smooth surface in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) and \(p \in M\). If \(u, v\) is any basis for the tangent plane \(T_p M\), then the Gaussian curvature at \(p\) is

\(\displaystyle K_p = rac{\langle R_p (u, v) v, u angle}{\langle u, u angle \langle v, v angle - \langle u, v angle^2} .\)

证明. Let \(e*1, e_2\) be an orthonormal basis for \(T_p M\) and suppose

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} u & = a e_1 + b e_2\ v & = c e_1 + d e_2 \end{array}\)

By the skew-symmetry of the curvature tensor,

\(\displaystyle R_p (u, v) = (a d - b c) R_p (e_1, e_2)\)

so that

\(\displaystyle \langle R_p (u, v) v, u angle = (a d - b c)^2 \langle R_p (e_1, e_2) e_2, e_1 angle .\)

On the other hand,

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \langle u, u angle \langle v, v angle - \langle u, v angle^2 & \ = (a^2 + b^2) (c^2 + d^2) & - (a c + b d)^2 = (a d - b c)^2 \end{array}\)

Hence,

\(\displaystyle K_p = \langle R_p (e_1, e_2) e_2, e_1 angle = rac{\langle R_p (u, v) v, u angle}{\langle u, u angle \langle v, v angle - \langle u, v angle^2} .\)
\(\Box\)

9Generalizations to Hypersurfaces in \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\)

9.1The Shape Operator of a Hypersurface

A hypersurface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) is a submanifold of codimension 1. Although all of our hypersurfaces are by definition smooth, sometimes for emphasis we adopt the redundant locution "smooth hypersurfaces". Assume that there is a smooth unit normal vector field \(N\) on \(M\); note that this is always possible locally on any hypersurface. By Proposition 4.3, we know that the directional derivative \(D\) on \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) is the Riemannian connection of \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\).

For any point \(p \in M\) and tangent vector \(X_p \in T_p M\), since \(\langle N, N angle \equiv 1\),

\(\displaystyle 0 = X_p \langle N, N angle = 2 \langle D*{X*p} N, N angle .\)

Therefore, \(D*{X*p} N\) is tangent to \(M\). The shape operator \(L_p : T_p M ightarrow T_p M\) is defined to be

\(\displaystyle L_p (X_p) = - D*{X*p} N \quad \operatorname{for}X_p \in T_p M.\)

Recall that \(\mathfrak{X} (M)\) is the vector space of \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields and \(\mathcal{F}= C^{\infty} (M)\) the ring of \(C^{\infty}\) functions on \(M\). As the point \(p\) varies over \(M\), the shape operator globalizes to an \(\mathcal{F}\)-linear map \(L : \mathfrak{X} (M) ightarrow \mathfrak{X} (M)\) given by \(L (X)\_p = L_p (X_p)\).

命题 9.1. Let \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) be \(C^{\infty}\) vector fields on an oriented hypersurface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\). Then

  1. \(\langle L (X), Y angle = \langle D_X Y, N angle\).

  2. the shape operator is self-adjoint with respect to the Euclidean metric inherited from \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\).

证明. Since \(\langle Y, N angle = 0\), by the compatibility of \(D\) with the metric,

\(\displaystyle 0 = X \langle Y, N angle = \langle D_X Y, N angle + \langle Y, D_X N angle .\)

Hence,

\(\displaystyle \langle D_X Y, N angle = \langle Y, - D_X N angle = \langle Y, L (X) angle .\) (9.1)

Reversing the roles of \(X\) and \(Y\) gives

\(\displaystyle \langle D_Y X, N angle = \langle X, L (Y) angle .\) (9.2)

Since \([X, Y]\) is tangent to \(M\),

\(\displaystyle \langle [X, Y], N angle = 0.\) (9.3)

By torsion-freeness, subtracting (9.2) and (9.3) from (9.1) gives

\(\displaystyle 0 = \langle D_X Y - D_Y X - [X, Y], N angle = \langle Y, L (X) angle - \langle X, L (Y) angle .\)
\(\Box\)

Since the shape operator \(L_p : T_p M ightarrow T_p M\) is self-adjoint, all of its eigenvalues \(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n\) are real. These are the principal curvatures of the hypersurface \(M\) at \(p\). We define the mean curvature of \(M\) at \(p\) to be the average of the principal curvature

\(\displaystyle H (p) = rac{1}{n} \sum*{i = 1}^n \lambda*i = rac{1}{n} \operatorname{tr} (L_p),\)

and the Gaussian curvature at \(p\) to be the determinant of the shape operator

\(\displaystyle K (p) = \prod*{i = 1}^n \lambda*i = \det (L_p) .\)

9.2The Riemannian Connection of a Hypersurface

We learned earlier that the unique Riemannian connection on \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) is the directional derivative \(D\).

定理 9.2. Let \(M\) be a smooth hypersurface in \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) and \(D\) the directional derivative on \(R^{n + 1}\). For \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), the tangential component of \(D_X Y\) defines the Riemannian connection \( abla\) of \(M\):

\(\displaystyle abla_X Y = (D_X Y)*{ an} .\)

证明. Since it is evident that \( abla\) satisfies the two defining properties of a connection, it suffices to check that \( abla*X Y\) is torsion-free and compatible with the metric.

  1. Torsion-freeness: Let \(T_D\) and \(T*{ abla}\) be the torsions of \(D\) and \( abla\), respectively. By definition, for \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\),

    \(\displaystyle D*X Y = abla_X Y + (D_X Y)*{\operatorname{nor}},\) (9.4)
    \(\displaystyle D*Y X = abla_Y X + (D_Y X)*{\operatorname{nor}} .\) (9.5)

    Since \(D\) is torsion-free,

    \(\displaystyle D*X Y - D_Y X = [X, Y] .\)

    Equating the normal components of both sides, we get \((D_X Y)*{\operatorname{nor}} - (D*Y X)*{\operatorname{nor}} = 0\). Therefore, by (9.4) and (9.5),

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} 0 = T*D (X, Y) & = D_X Y - D_Y X - [X, Y]\ & = ( abla_X Y - abla_Y X - [X, Y]) + (D_X Y)*{\operatorname{nor}} - (D*Y X)*{\operatorname{nor}}\ & = abla*X Y - abla_Y X - [X, Y] = T*{ abla} (X, Y) . \end{array}\)

  2. Compatibility with the metric: For \(X, Y, Z \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\),

    \(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} X \langle Y, Z angle & = \langle D*X Y, Z angle + \langle Y, D_X Z angle\ & = \langle abla_X Y + (D_X Y)*{\operatorname{nor}}, Z angle + \langle Y, abla*X Z + ( abla_X Y)*{\operatorname{nor}} angle\ & = \langle abla_X Y, Z angle + \langle Y, abla_X Z angle . \end{array}\)

Therefore, \( abla_X Y\) is the Riemannian connection on \(E\)(?M).\(\Box\)

9.3The Second Fundamental Form

At each point \(p\) of an oriented hypersurface \(M \subset \mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\), there is a sequence of symmetric bilinear maps on \(T_p M\) called the first, second and third fundamental forms, and so on:

\(\displaystyle egin{array}{rl} \mathrm{I} (X_p, Y_p) & = \langle X_p, Y_p angle\ \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (X_p, Y_p) & = \langle L (X_p), Y_p angle = \langle X_p, L (Y_p) angle\ \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (X_p, Y_p) & = \langle L^2 (X_p), Y_p angle = \langle L (X_p), L (Y_p) angle = \langle X_p, L^2 (Y_p) angle, \ldots \end{array}\)

For \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), the directional derivative \(D*X Y\) decompose into the sum of tangential component and a normal component. The tangential component \((D_X Y)*{ an}\) is the Riemannian connection on \(M\); the normal component \((D*X Y)*{\operatorname{nor}}\) is essentially the second fundamental form.

命题 9.3. If \(N\) is a smooth unit normal vector field on the hypersurface \(M\) in \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) and \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\) are smooth vector fields on \(M\), then

\(\displaystyle (D*X Y)*{\operatorname{nor}} = \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (X, Y) N.\)

9.4The Gauss Curvature and Codazzi-Mainardi Equations

Let \(M\) be an oriented, smooth hypersurface in \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\), with a smooth unit normal vector field \(N\). The relation between the Riemannian connection \(D\) on \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) and the Riemannian connection on \(M\) implies a relation between the curvature \(R_D (X, Y) Z = 0\) on \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) and the curvature \(R (X, Y) Z\) on \(M\). The tangential component of this relation gives the Gauss curvature equation, and the normal component gives the Codazzi-Mainardi equation.

定理 9.4. If \(M\) is an oriented, smooth hypersurface in \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) and \(X, Y, Z \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), then

  1. (the Gauss curvature equation)

    \(\displaystyle R (X, Y) = \langle L (Y), Z angle L (X) - \langle L (X), Z angle L (Y) .\)

  2. (the Codazzi-Mainardi equation)

    \(\displaystyle abla_X L (Y) - abla_Y L (X) - L ([X, Y]) = 0.\)

推论 9.5. If \(M\) is a smooth oriented hypersurface in \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) with curvature \(R\) and \(X, Y \in \mathfrak{X} (M)\), then

\(\displaystyle \langle R (X, Y) Y, X angle = \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (X, X) \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (Y, Y) - \mathrm{I} \mathrm{I} (X, Y)^2 .\)
entry.summary: chapter 1 of Differential Geometry, Connection, Curvature and Characteristic Classes

entry.title: The founding fathers of optimal transport, Cédric Villani

link.title: The founding fathers of optimal transport, Cédric Villani

entry.content:
3The founding fathers of optimal transport

和数学中的其他研究主题一样，最优传输诞生过多次。第一次发生在十八世纪末，由法国几何学家Gaspard Monge提出。

Monge 1746年出生在 French Ancient Régime. 由于他出色的技能，被一所陆军训练学校录取。他独立发明了 descriptive geometry(画法几何)，这一方法如此强大的力量使得他在22岁被授予教授，让他的理论保持为军事秘密，仅供高级军官们使用。他后来是法国大革命最热心的战士科学家之一，在多个政权下担任教授，在恐怖统治期间逃过了一个死刑，成为了拿破仑最好的朋友之一。他在巴黎高师和理工学院教书。他的大部分工作投入到几何中。

在1781年，他发表了著名的工作之一，Mémoire sur la théorie des déblais et des remblais. （déblais 意思是挖掘，remblai意思是填充构造）...我们想最小化总的传输代价。Monge 假设一单位质量的传输代价由质量和距离的乘积给出。

今天法国巴黎有一条Monge 街， 人们在那里可以找到一家优秀的面包店叫作 Le Boulanger de Monge. 为了承认这一点，并展示Monge的问题可以如何从经济学角度叙述，我将如下叙述这个问题。考虑一大群烘焙店，生产面包，需在每天早上运到咖啡店供消费者食用。每个咖啡店要消耗的面包数提前知道，可以被建模为特定空间上的概率测度（有一个“生产密度”和“消耗密度”）。问题是每单位的面包应该去哪里来最小化传输代价。所以Monge的问题其实是寻找一个 optimal coupling; 更精确地说，他在寻找一个 deterministic optimal coupling。

Monge在三维空间中连续的质量分布上研究了他的问题。在他优美的几何直觉指引下，他作出重要观察发现传输需要沿着直线进行而且会和一族曲面正交。这个研究引领他发现了曲率的线，一个本身对曲面的几何就是重要贡献的概念。他的想法后来由 Charles Dupin和Paul Appell继续发展。在现代数学的标准下，这些论点都是有缺陷的，但显然值得用现代工具重新审视这些问题。

在很久之后，Monge的问题被俄罗斯数学家 Leonid Vitaliyevich Kantorovich 重新发现。1912年出生的 Kantorovich 也是一个非常有天分的数学家，在18岁时就被认可为一流的研究者，并在跟Monge 一样的年纪获得教授职位。他在数学的很多领域研究，尤其在经济学的应用以及后来的理论计算机科学上有强烈兴趣。在1938年，一个实验室咨询他一个特定优化问题的解，他发现这是在不同经济领域中都出现的一整类线性规划问题的代表。在这个发现的驱动下，他发展了线性规划的工具，后来在经济学中变得著名。他的最重要的部分工作的发表被延后了，因为苏联当局对关于经济学的研究成果的泄密处理得非常谨慎。事实上（这和Monge是另一个共同点）很多年Kantorovich被禁止公开讨论一些他的主要发现。最终他的工作变得广为人知，然后他在1975年和 Tjalling Koopmans 一起获得诺贝尔经济学奖，“for their contributions to the theory of optimum allocation of resources”.

（Kantorovich独立创立了线性规划方法，提出了乘子，Koopmans在经济学体系中赋予了具体的含义，即著名的“影子价格”。两人的研究结合在一起，确立了如何将稀缺资源进行最优分配的数学与经济学基础。）

对我们直接感兴趣的部分，即optimal coupling 来说，Kantorovich 通过泛函分析工具，描述和证明了，一个对偶定理，其在之后还会起到重要作用。他还推导了概率测度之间的一个方便的距离的记号：两个测度之间的距离应该是最优传输代价。概率测度之间的这个距离今天被叫做 Kantorovich-Rubinstein 距离，且已经被证明尤其灵活有用。

在作出主要成果仅过了几年之后，Kantorovich 和Monge的工作建立起了联系。从此，optimal coupling 的问题被叫做 Monge-Kantorovich 问题。

在二十世纪的后半部分，最优coupling的技巧和 Kantorovich-Rubinstein 距离（今天通常被叫做 Wasserstein 距离，或其他名称）被统计学家和概率学家使用。“基”空间可以是有限维，或是无穷维：例如，在路径空间的概率测度上给出有趣的距离的定义。七十年代值得注意的贡献有 Roland Dobrushin，用这样的距离来研究粒子系统；Hiroshi Tananka，用它们研究 Boltzmann 方程的简单变体的时变行为。八十年代中期，这方面的专家，像 Svetlozar Rachev 或 Ludger Ruschendorf， 拥有最优传输相关的一大批想法，工具，技巧和应用。

在那期间，reparameterization techniques (change of variables 的另一个名字)在被很多研究人员用在研究体积或积分的不等式上。直到后来人们才意识到最优传输往往提供了有用的重参数化。

八十年代末，三个方向几乎同时独立出现，彻底改变了最优传输的整个面貌。

一个是 John Mather's 的 Lagrangian dynamical systems上的工作。最小化作用量的曲线是动力系统中的基本重要对象，而构造满足特定数量条件的封闭最小化作用量的曲线是一个经典问题。在八十年代末之前，Mather 发现在研究最小作用量曲线之外，相空间的最小作用量静态测度非常方便。Mather的测度是最小作用量曲线的一个推广，它们解决了一个变分问题，实际上是一个 Monge-Kantorovich 问题。在拉格朗日量的一些条件下，Mather证明了一个值得称道的结果（大致来说）是特定最小作用量测度自动集中在Lipschitgz图上。我们在第8章将会明白，这个问题和确定性最优coupling的构造密切相关。

第二个研究方向来自 Yann Brenier的工作。在研究不可压缩流体力学的问题时，Brenier需要构造一个算子，其可以在开集的保测度映射上像投影一样作用（在概率语言下，保测度映射是Lebesgue测度到自身的确定性coupling）。他明白他可以通过引入一个最优coupling来实现这一点：If \(u\) is the map for which one wants to compute the projection, introduce a coupling of the Lebesgue measure \(\mathcal{L}\) with \(u_{\#} \mathcal{L}\). 这一研究揭示了一个人们没有预料到的最优传输和流体力学间的联系；同时，在指出和Monge-Ampere方程之间的联系后，Brenier吸引到了PDE领域研究的注意。

第三个研究方向，也是最令人吃惊的，来自数学之外。Mike Cullen was part of a group of meteorologists with a well-developed mathematical taste, working on semi-geostrophic equations, sused in meteorology for the modeling of atmospheric fronts. Cullen and his collaborators showd that a certain famous change of unknown due to Brian Hoskins could be re-interpreted in terms of an optimal coupling problem, and they identified the minimizaiton property as a stability condition. A striking outcome of the work was that optimal transport could arise naturally in partial differential equations which seemed to have nothing to do with it.

All three contributions emphasized (in their respective domain) that important information can be gained by a qualitative description of optimal transport. These new directions of research attracted various mathematicians (among the first, Luis Caffarelli, Craig Evans, Wilfrid Gangbo, Robert McCann, and others), who worked on a better description of the structure of optimal transport and found other applications.

An important conceptual step was accomplished by Felix Otto, who discovered an appealing formalism introducing a differential point of view in optimal transport theory. This opened the way to a more geometric description of the space of probability measures, and connected optimal transport to the theory of diffusion equations, thus leading to a rich interplay of geometry, functional analysis and partial differential equations.

今天（2008～2009）最优传输已经成为一个蓬勃发展的industry，包括许多研究者和许多趋势。除了 meterology，流体力学和扩散方程，它也被应用到多样的领域，比如沙堆坍塌，图像匹配，网络和反射天线的设计。我的书，Topics in Optimal Transport，大概在2000到2003年之间写就，是第一次尝试提供一个现代的完整理论视图。在那之后，这个领域发展的比我想象的更快，且它从没有如今天这么活跃。

（事实上，可能活跃到了今天2026年，尤其在机器学习领域OT理论和应用还在发展。）
entry.summary: part of chapter 3 of Optimal Transport, Old and New

entry.title: Three example of coupling techniques, Cédric Villani

link.title: Three example of coupling techniques, Cédric Villani

entry.content:
Optimal Transport, Old and New

by Cédric Villani

2Three examples of coupling techniques

Convergence of the Langevin process

Consider a particle subject to the force induced by a potential \(V \in C^1 (\mathbb{R}^n)\), a friction and a random white noise agitation. If \(X_t\) stands for the position of the particle at time \(t\), \(m\) for its mass, \(\lambda\) for the friction coefficient, \(k\) for the Boltzmann constant and \(T\) for the temperature of the heat bath, the Newton's equation of motion can be written

\(\displaystyle m rac{d^2 X_t}{d t^2} = - abla V (X_t) - \lambda m rac{d X_t}{d t} + \sqrt{k T} rac{d B_t}{d t},\) (2.1)

where \((B_t)_{t \geq 0}\) is a standard Brownian motion. This is a second order (stochastic) differential equaiton, so it should come with initial conditions for both the position \(X\) and the velocity \(\dot{X}\).

Now consider a large cloud of particles evolving independently, according to (2.1); the question is whether the distribution of particles will converge to a definite limit as \(t ightarrow \infty\). In other words: Consider the stochastic differential equation (2.1) starting from some intial distribution \(\mu_0 (d x d v) =\operatorname{law} (X_0, \dot{X_0})\); is it true that law(\(X_t\)), or law(\(X_t, \dot{X_t}\)) will converge to some given limit law as \(t ightarrow \infty\)?

Obviously, to solve this problem, one has to make some assumptions on the potential \(V\), which should prevent the particles from all escaping at infinity; for instance, we can make the very strong assumption that \(V\) is uniformly convex, i.e. there exists \(K > 0\) such that the Hessian \( abla^2 V\) satisfies \( abla^2 V \geq K I_n\). Some assumption on the initial distribution might also be needed: for instance, it is natural to assume that the Hamiltonian has finite expectation at initial time:

\(\displaystyle \mathbb{E} \left( V (X_0) + rac{| \dot{X} |^2}{2} ight) < + \infty\)

Under these assumptions, it is true that there is exponential convergence to equilibrium, at least if \(V\) does not grow too wildly at infinity (for instance if the Hessian of \(V\) is also bounded above). However, I do not know of any simple method to prove this.

On the other hand, consider the limit where the friction coefficient is quite strong, and the motion of the particle is so slow that the acceleration term may be neglected in front of the others: then, up to resetting units, equation (2.1) becomes

\(\displaystyle rac{d X_t}{d t} = - abla V (X_t) + \sqrt{2} rac{d B_t}{d t},\) (2.2)

which is often called a Langevin process. Now, to study the convergence of equilibrium for (2.2) there is an extremely simple solution by coupling. Consider another random position \((Y_t)_{t \geq 0}\) obeying the same equation as (2.2):

\(\displaystyle rac{d Y_t}{d t} = - abla V (Y_t) + \sqrt{2} rac{d B_t}{d t} .\) (2.3)

where the random realization of the Brownian motion is the same as in (2.2) (this is the coupling). The initial positions \(X_0\) and \(Y_0\) may be coupled in an arbitrary way, but it is possible to assume that they are independent. In any case, since they are driven by the same Brownian motion, \(X_t\) and \(Y_t\) will be correlated for \(t > 0\).

Since \(B_t\) is not differentiable as a function of time, neighther \(X_t\) nor \(Y_t\) is differentiable (equations (2.2) and (2.3) hold only in the sense of solutions of stochastic differential equations); but it is easily checked that \(\alpha_t := X_t - Y_t\) is a continuously differentiable function of time, and

\(\displaystyle rac{d \alpha_t}{d t} = - ( abla V (X_t) - abla V (Y_t)),\)
\(\displaystyle rac{d}{d t} rac{| \alpha_t |^2}{2} = - \langle abla V (X_t) - abla V (Y_t), X_t - Y_t angle \leq - K | X_t - Y_t |^2 = - K | \alpha_t |^2 .\)

It follows by Gronwalls lemma that

\(\displaystyle | \alpha_t |^2 \leq e^{- 2 K t} | \alpha_0 |^2 .\)

Assume for simplicity that \(\mathbb{E} | X_0 |^2\) and \(\mathbb{E} | Y_0 |^2\) are finite. Then

\(\displaystyle \mathbb{E} | X_t - Y_t |^2 \leq e^{- 2 K t} \mathbb{E} | X_0 - Y_0 |^2 \leq 2 (\mathbb{E} | X_0 |^2 +\mathbb{E} | Y_0 |^2) e^{- 2 K t} .\) (2.4)

In particular, \(X_t - Y_t\) converges to 0 almost surely, and this is independent of the distribution of \(Y_0\).

This in itself would be essentially sufficient to guarantee the existence of a stationary distribution: but in any case, it is easy to check, by applying the diffusion formula, that

\(\displaystyle u (d y) = rac{e^{- V (y)} d y}{Z}\)

(where \(Z = \int e^{- V}\) is a normalization constant) is staionary: If \(\operatorname{law} (Y_0) = u\), then also \(\operatorname{law} (Y_t) = u\). Then (2.4) easily implies that \(\mu_t := \operatorname{law} (X_t) \) converges weakly to \( u\); in addition, the convergence is exponentially fast.

Euclidean isoperimetry
skipped.

Caffarelli's log-concave perturbation theorem
skipped
entry.summary: part of chapter 2 of Optimal Transport, Old and New

entry.title: The Monge-Ampère Equation, Connor Mooney
entry.content:
The Monge-Ampère Equation

by Connor Mooney

2018

1Introduction

Discuss the basic properties of Alexandrov solutions to the Monge-Ampère equation.

We then discuss the interior and boundary regularity for Alexandrov solutions to \(\det D^2 u = 1\).

2Motivation

The Monge-Ampère equation

\(\displaystyle \det D^2 u = f (x, u, abla u)\)

for a convex function \(u\) on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) arises in several interesting applications.

2.1Prescribed Gauss Curvature

The Gauss curvature \(K (x)\) of the graph of a function \(u\) on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) at \((x, u (x))\) is given by

\(\displaystyle \det D^2 u = K (x) (1 + | abla u |^2)^{ rac{n + 2}{2}}\)

It is a good exercise to derive this formula.

高斯曲率 \(K\) 的定义是形变算子（Shape Operator）的行列式，也可以表示为第二基本形式的行列式与第一基本形式的行列式之比：

\(\displaystyle K = rac{\det (h_{ij})}{\det (g_{ij})}\)

参数化与切空间

将函数 \(u : \mathbb{R}^n o \mathbb{R}\) 的图像看作 \(\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}\) 中的一个 \(n\) 维超曲面，其参数化映射可以写为：

\(\displaystyle X (x) = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n, u (x))\)

对每个坐标 \(x_i\) 求偏导，得到切空间的基向量：

\(\displaystyle X_i = rac{\partial X}{\partial x_i} = (0, \ldots, 1, \ldots, 0, u_i) = e_i + u_i e_{n + 1}\)

其中 \(u_i = rac{\partial u}{\partial x_i}\) 是 \(u\) 的偏导数，\(e_i\) 是 \(\mathbb{R}^n\) 的标准基向量。

计算第一基本形式（度量张量）

第一基本形式的系数 \(g_{ij}\) 是切向量的内积：

\(\displaystyle g_{ij} = \langle X_i, X_j angle = \delta_{ij} + u_i u_j\)

为了求出矩阵 \((g_{ij})\) 的行列式 \(\det (g_{ij})\)，我们可以利用矩阵行列式引理（Matrix Determinant Lemma），即 \(\det (I + vv^T) = 1 + |v|^2\)。在这里，该矩阵可以表示为单位矩阵加上梯度向量的特征线性组合，因此：

\(\displaystyle \det (g_{ij}) = 1 + | abla u|^2\)

计算单位法向量

超曲面的单位法向量 \( u\) 需要同时垂直于所有的切向量 \(X_i\)，且长度为 1。容易验证，未归一化的法向量可以取为 \((- abla u, 1)\)，因为：

\(\displaystyle \langle X_i, (- abla u, 1) angle = - u_i + u_i = 0\)

对其进行归一化后，得到单位法向量：

\(\displaystyle u = rac{(- abla u, 1)}{\sqrt{1 + | abla u|^2}}\)

计算第二基本形式

对切向量再次求导，得到二阶偏导数位置向量：

\(\displaystyle X_{i j} = rac{\partial^2 X}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} = (0, 0, \ldots, 0, u_{i j})\)

其中 \(u_{ij} = rac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}\) 是 Hessian 矩阵 \(D^2 u\) 的元素。

第二基本形式的系数 \(h_{ij}\) 定义为 \(X_{ij}\) 在法方向 \( u\) 上的投影：

\(\displaystyle h_{ij} = \langle X_{ij}, u angle = rac{u_{ij}}{\sqrt{1 + | abla u|^2}}\)

写成矩阵形式，第二基本形式矩阵为：

\(\displaystyle H = rac{D^2 u}{\sqrt{1 + | abla u|^2}}\)

因为 \(H\) 是一个 \(n imes n\) 的矩阵，当提取常数标量因子时，行列式需要提取该因子的 \(n\) 次方：

\(\displaystyle \det (h_{ij}) = \det \left( rac{D^2 u}{\sqrt{1 + | abla u|^2}} ight) = rac{\det D^2 u}{(1 + | abla u|^2)^{n / 2}}\)

结合高斯曲率定义

根据微分几何，超曲面的高斯曲率 \(K (x)\) 等于：

\(\displaystyle K = rac{\det (h_{ij})}{\det (g_{ij})}\)

将步骤 2 和步骤 4 中得到的行列式代入上式中：

\(\displaystyle K = rac{\det D^2 u}{(1 + | abla u |^2)^{(n + 2) / 2}}\)

2.2Optimal Transport

Given probability densities \(f, g\) supported on domains \(\Omega_f, \Omega_g\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\), the optimal transport problem asks to minimize the transport cost

\(\displaystyle J (T) = \int_{\Omega_f} | T (x) - x |^2 f (x) d x\)

over measure-preserving maps \(T : \Omega_f ightarrow \Omega_g\) (that is \(f (x) d x = g (T (x)) \det D T (x) d x\)). An important theorem of Brenier says that the optimal map exists, and is given by the gradient of a convex function \(u\) on \(\Omega_f\). The measure-preserving condition implies

\(\displaystyle \det D^2 u = rac{f (x)}{g ( abla u (x))},\)

in a certain weak sense.

2.3Fluid Dynamics

Large-scale fluid flows in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) are modeled by a system of evolution equations for a probability density \( ho (x, t)\) and a function \(u (x, t)\) that is convex in \(x\) for all \(t\). The system is

\(\displaystyle \left\{ egin{array}{l} \partial_t ho + (x - abla u)^{ ot} \cdot abla ho = 0\ \det D^2 u = ho . \end{array} ight.\)

Here \(w^{ ot}\) denotes the counter-clockwise rotation of \(w\) by \( rac{\pi}{2}\). This can be viewed as a fully nonlinear version of the incompressible Euler equations in 2\(D\), where the Monege-Ampère operator repalces the Laplace operator.

3Weak Solutions

We introduce a useful notion of weak solution based on the idea of polyhedral approximations. We then solve the Dirichlet problem on bounded domains. Detailed expositions of these topics can be found in work of Cheng-Yau, and in the books of Gutierrez and Figalli.

3.1Alexandrov Solutions

If \(v\) is a \(C^2\) convex function on \(\mathbb{R}^n\), then the area formula gives

\(\displaystyle \int_{\Omega} \det D^2 v d x = | abla v (\Omega) | .\)

For an arbitrary convex function \(v\) on a domain \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n\) and \(E \subset \Omega\) we define

\(\displaystyle M v (E) = | \partial v (E) |,\)

where \(\partial v (E)\) is the set of slopes of supporting hyperplanes to the graph of \(v\) (the sub-gradients of \(v\)) over points in \(E\). We have ([F], Theorem 2.3)

命题 1. \(M v\) is a Borel measure on \(\Omega\).

We call \(M v\) the Monge-Ampère measure of \(v\).

It is easy to check that if \(v \in C^2\), then \(M v = \det D^2 v d x\). A more interesting example is the polyhedral graph

\(\displaystyle v = \max_{1 \leq i \leq 3} \{ p_i \cdot x \}\)

over \(\mathbb{R}^2\). The set \(\partial v (0)\) is the (closed) triangle with vertices \(\{ p_i \}\). The sub-gradients of the "edges" of the graph are the segments joining \(p_i\), and the sub-gradients of the "faces" are \(p_i\). Thus, \(M v\) is a Dirac mass at 0 with weight given by the area of the triangle.

定义 1. Let \(\mu\) be a Borel measure on a domain \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n\). We say that a convex function \(u\) on \(\Omega\) is an Alexandrov solution of \(\det D^2 u = \mu\) if \(M u = \mu\).

The key fact that is used to prove Proposition 1, and is essential for many other parts of the theory, is ([F], Lemma A.30):

命题 2. Let \(v\) be a convex function on a domain \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n\). Then

\(\displaystyle | \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^n : p \in \partial v (x) \cap \partial v (y) \operatorname{for}\operatorname{some}x eq y \in \Omega \} | = 0.\)

3.2Maximum Principle and Compactness

Alexandrov solutions are useful because they satisfy a maximum principle and have good compactness properties.

We first observe that if \(u\) and \(v\) are convex on a bounded domain \(\Omega\), with \(u = v\) on \(\partial \Omega\) and \(u \leq v\) in \(\Omega\), then

\(\displaystyle \partial v (\Omega) \subset \partial u (\Omega) .\)

From this observation one concludes the comparison principle ([F], Theorem 2.10):

命题 3. Assume \(u\) and \(v\) are convex on a bounded domain \(\Omega\), with \(u = v\) on \(\partial \Omega\). If \(M u \geq M v\) in \(\Omega\), then \(u \leq v\) in \(\Omega\).

Alexandrov maximum principle ([F], Theorem 2.8):

命题 4. If \(u\) is convex on a bounded convex domain \(\Omega\) and \(u |_{\partial \Omega} = 0\), then

\(\displaystyle | u (x) | \leq C (n, \operatorname{diam} (\Omega), M u (\Omega)) \operatorname{dist}. (x, \partial \Omega)^{1 / n} .\)

This says that functions with bounded Monge-Ampère mass have a \(C^{1 / n}\) modulus of continuity near the boundary of a sub level set, that depends only on rough geometric properties of this set.

证明思路略去。

The other important property of Alexandrov solutions is closedness under uniform convergence. That is:

命题 5. If \(u_k\) converge uniformly to \(u\) in \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n\), then \(M u_k\) converges weakly to \(M u\) in \(\Omega\).

For a detailed proof see [F], Proposition 2.6.

As a consequence of Proposition 4 and 5 we have the compactness of solutions with fixed linear boundary data:

命题 6. For a bounded convex domain \(\Omega\), the collection of functions

\(\displaystyle \mathcal{A}= \{ v\operatorname{convex}\operatorname{on} \Omega, v |_{\partial \Omega} = 0, M v (\Omega) \leq C_0 \}\)

is compact. That is, any sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) has a uniformly convergent subsequence whose Monge-Ampère measures converge weakly to that of the limit.

3.3Dirichlet Problem

We conclude this section by discussing the Dirichlet problem.

定理 1. Let \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n\) be a bounded strictly convex domain, \(\mu\) a bounded Borel measure on \(\Omega\), and \(\varphi \in C (\partial \Omega)\). Then there exists a unique Alexandrov solution in \(C ( ar{\Omega})\) to the Dirichlet problem

\(\displaystyle \left\{ egin{array}{ll} \det D^2 u = \mu & \operatorname{in} \Omega\ u |_{\partial \Omega} = \varphi . & \end{array} ight.\)

Sketch of Proof
Refer the reader to [CY] for details.

注意 1. When \(\varphi\) is linear, we don't require strict convexity of \(\partial \Omega\). The strict convexity is necessary for general \(\varphi\) since no convex function can continuously attain e.g. the boundary data \(- | x |^2\) when \(\partial \Omega\) has flat pieces.

The strict convexity of \(\partial \Omega\) is used in the last step. It guarantees that for any subset \(\{ y_i \}_{i = 1}^M\) of \(\partial \Omega\), each \(y_k\) is a vertex of the convex hull of \(\{ y_i \}_{i = 1}^M\).

Closely related is the fact that when \(\partial \Omega\) is strictly convex, the convex envelope of the graph of \(\varphi\) (that is, the supremum of linear functions beneath the graph) continuously achieves the boundary data, and has 0 Monge-Ampère measure.

entry.title: Chap 2 The Theory of Pfaffian Forms, Cartan

link.title: Chap 2 The Theory of Pfaffian Forms, Cartan

entry.content:
1The Theory of Pfaffian Forms

我：Cartan 的 Riemannian geometry in an orthogonal frame书中，第二章 The theory of Pfaffian forms 在讲什么？为什么要引入两种微分符号d 和 δ？然后假设他们可交换，然后后面的推导说对函数微分也可交换以及不依赖于独立变量的选择是什么意义？

Gemini (Thinking):

艾黎·嘉当（Éli Cartan）的经典著作《Leçons sur la géométrie des espaces de Riemann》（活动标架法下的黎曼几何）第二章 “The theory of Pfaffian forms”（Pfaff 型/全微分形式理论），是整本书的“数学发动机”。

嘉当之所以要在这里大费周章地引入 \(d\) 和 \(\delta\)，并讨论它们的交换性与独立性，是为了在还没有现代“流形”和“坐标无关（Coordinate-free）”抽象语言的时代，凭空建立起一套能够跨越坐标系限制的微分几何工具——也就是现代人所说的“外微分（Exterior Derivative）”和“不变性（Invariance）”。

下面我们逐一拆解你的疑问：

—

1. 第二章到底在讲什么？

简而言之，这一章在讲外微分形式的代数与分析基础。 在第一章引入了活动标架（Moving Frames）之后，几何问题变成了处理一系列形如 \(\omega = \sum A_i dx_i\) 的线性微分形式（即 Pfaff 型，现代称为 1-form）。

为了研究这些形式的几何性质（比如：如何判断空间是否弯曲？标架如何旋转？），嘉当需要知道如何对这些微分形式求导。直接求偏导数会产生一堆极其恶心的坐标变换项。因此，第二章的核心目的就是引入外微分（Exterior differentiation）的机制，为后面推导结构方程（Structure Equations）和曲率张量打下工具基础。

—

2. 为什么要引入两种微分符号 \(d\) 和 \(\delta\)？

在现代微分几何中，我们定义外微分 \(d \omega\) 是直接作用在两个向量场 \(X, Y\) 上的。但在嘉当的时代，最直观的办法是“算两次”。

想象你在流形（或曲面）上的一点。为了探测这个点周围的微观结构，你从这个点出发，沿着两个完全独立、互不干扰的方向进行无穷小位移：

符号 \(d\) 代表沿着第一个方向的微分位移。 符号 \(\delta\) 代表沿着第二个方向的微分位移。

引入两个符号，本质上是为了在空间中勾勒出一个无穷小的二维平行四边形。

当我们将一个 Pfaff 型 \(\omega\) 作用在这两个位移上时，我们会得到两个标量：\(\omega (d)\) 和 \(\omega (\delta)\)。嘉当通过研究这两者在对方方向上的变化率（即 \(\delta [\omega (d)] - d [\omega (\delta)]\)），来定义什么是“外微分”。这就好比在多变量微积分中，你要定义旋度（Curl）或混合偏导数，必须要同时沿着 \(x\) 轴和 \(y\) 轴各走一步一样。

—

3. 为什么要假设它们可交换（\(d \delta = \delta d\)）？

嘉当在书里假设，对于作为独立变量的坐标 \(x_i\)，有：

\(\displaystyle d (\delta x_i) = \delta (dx_i)\)

这个假设的几何意义是：这个无穷小平行四边形是闭合的。 也就是说，先沿着 \(d\) 走再沿着 \(\delta\) 走，与先沿着 \(\delta\) 走再沿着 \(d\) 走，最终到达的是同一个终点。

在数学上，这对应着我们最熟悉的偏导数可交换性（Clairaut's Theorem）。如果这两个位移是由两个独立的参数（比如参数曲面上的 \(u\) 和 \(v\)）生成的，那么：

\(d\) 对应 \(\frac{\partial}{\partial u} du\) , \(\delta\) 对应 \(\frac{\partial}{\partial v} dv\)

由于 \(\frac{\partial^2 x_i}{\partial u \partial v} = \frac{\partial^2 x_i}{\partial v \partial u}\)，自然就有 \(d (\delta x_i) = \delta (dx_i)\)。嘉当直接将其作为基本算子的代数性质提出来，是为了脱离具体参数的束缚。

—

4. 后续推导的深层意义

你提到后面推导出“对函数微分也可交换（\(d \delta f = \delta df\)）”，并且“不依赖于独立变量的选择”，这两点是全章的灵魂所在。

① 对任意函数 \(d \delta f = \delta df\) 的意义

如果 \(x_i\) 是独立变量（自变量），我们强行定义了 \(d \delta x_i = \delta dx_i\)。但如果 \(f (x_1, \ldots, x_n)\) 是一个因变量（函数），它的微分为：

\(\displaystyle \delta f = \sum \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \delta x_i\)

当我们再对它施加 \(d\) 算子时，根据乘积法则会产生两部分：一部分是二阶偏导数，另一部分包含 \(d \delta x_i\)。

经过严格推导，你会发现正是因为混合偏导数可交换以及自变量的 \(d \delta = \delta d\)，才使得任意函数 \(f\) 都能完美保持 \(d \delta f = \delta df\)。 这证明了这种算子交换律具有自洽性，它不是只对最简单的坐标变量有效，而是对空间 spray 出的任何标量场都有效。

② 不依赖于独立变量的选择（核心意义：坐标不变性）

这是最关键的一步。在几何中，我们经常要换坐标系（从 \(x\) 坐标系换到 \(y\) 坐标系）。

在旧坐标系中，\(x_i\) 是独立变量，我们假设了 \(d \delta x_i = \delta dx_i\)。 当我们换到新坐标系时，\(y_j\) 变成了 \(x_i\) 的函数。那么在新坐标系下，\(d \delta y_j\) 还等于 \(\delta dy_j\) 吗？

由于前面已经证明了“对任意函数都有 \(d \delta f = \delta df\)”，而新坐标 \(y_j = y_j (x)\) 本质上就是旧坐标的函数，因此立刻可以得到：

\(\displaystyle d \delta y_j = \delta dy_j\)

这个结论的几何意义是极其震撼的：它说明 \(d \delta = \delta d\) 这个性质不依赖于你站在哪个坐标系下观察。它是流形本身固有的一种“几何结构”，而不是因为你选了某个特殊坐标系才凑巧成立的。

最终目的：为了定义“外微分”

有了上述基础，嘉当就可以定义一个 Pfaff 型 \(\omega\) 的外微分了。他考虑这样一个表达式：

\(\displaystyle \Omega = \delta \omega (d) - d \omega (\delta)\)

因为 \(d\) 和 \(\delta\) 在函数和变量上都可交换，经过化简后，所有包含二阶微分 \(d \delta x_i\) 的项全部消掉了，只剩下：

\(\displaystyle \Omega = \sum_{i, j} \left( \frac{\partial A_j}{\partial x_i} - \frac{\partial A_i}{\partial x_j} \right) dx_i \delta x_j\)

这个 \(\Omega\) 就是现代语言里的 外微分 \(d \omega\) 作用在两个向量上的结果。正因为前面证明了交换性不依赖于变量的选择，所以这个外微分 \(\Omega\) 表现出完美的协变性（Covariance）——无论你用什么坐标系去算它，算出来的几何实体都是同一个。

通过这种精妙的代数假设，嘉当成功地在没有现代流形和切丛语言的限制下，建立了一套完全独立于坐标选择的微分形式体系，并直接引出了后续研究黎曼曲率的联络形式与结构方程。

下面是本章主要内容和结论。

We return to an orthonormal frame. We say that if a frame motion is given, i.e., the position vector \(M\) and the unit axial vectors \(I_i\) are given as functions of the parameters \(u^{\alpha}\), then we can find the components \(\omega^i\) and \(\omega_i^j\) of an infinitesimal displacement of a frame.

A problem arises: could six arbitrarily given forms \(\omega^i\) and \(\omega_i^j\) which are linear combinations of \(d u^{\alpha}\) represent a displacement of a trihedron in an ordinary Euclidean space?

In order to answer this question, we make a digression into analysis and consider properties of Pfaffian forms.

1Differentiating in a given direction.

Suppose that a Pfaffian form

\(\displaystyle \omega = a_1 d x^1 + a_2 d x^2 + \cdots + a_n d x^n\)

is given. Usually the differentials \(d x^1, \ldots, d x^n\) are considered as new independent variables. However, we can also take them to be given functions of the independent variables,

\(\displaystyle d x^i = \xi^i (x^1, \ldots, x^n)\)

(a directed displacement with a definite symbol of differentiation). Consider two symbols of differentiation \(d\) and \(\delta\) and two series of differentials

\(\displaystyle \begin{array}{rl} d \delta x^1, d \delta x^2, \ldots, d \delta x^n & ;\\ \delta d x^1, \delta d x^2, \ldots, \delta d x^n & , \end{array}\)

which in general are distinct.

We assume that these two symbols of differentiation commute,

\(\displaystyle d \delta x^i = \delta d x^i .\) (1)

定理 1. If the symbols of differentiation \(d\) and \(\delta\) commute for independent variables, then they also commute for functions.

证明. In fact, consider a function

\(\displaystyle f (x^1, \ldots, x^n) .\)

Then

\(\displaystyle d f = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x^i} d x^i, \delta f = \frac{\delta f}{\partial x^i} \delta x^i .\)

The second differentiation gives

\(\displaystyle \begin{array}{rl} \delta d f = & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^i \partial x^k} d x^i \delta x^k + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x^i} \delta d x^i,\\ d \delta f = & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^i \partial x^k} \delta x^i d x^k + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x^i} d \delta x^i . \hspace{3cm} \text{(2)} \end{array}\)

By condition (1), the second terms of these two expressions are the same.\(\Box\)

\(\displaystyle \delta d f = d \delta f.\) (3)

推论 2. The property of commutativity for two symbols of differentiation \(d\) and \(\delta\) does not depend of the choice of independent variables.

Bilinear covariant of Frobenius.
Consider a Pfaffian form

\(\displaystyle \omega (d) = a_i d x^i,\)

and compose an expression

\(\displaystyle d w (\delta) - \delta d \omega (d) .\)

Differentiating \(\omega (d)\) by means of differentiation \(\delta\), we obtain

\(\displaystyle \delta \omega (d) = \delta a_i d x^i + a_i \delta d x^i,\)

and similarly

\(\displaystyle d \omega (\delta) = d a_i \delta x^i + a_i d \delta x^i .\)

Subtracting the first expression from the second, we find that

\(\displaystyle d \omega (\delta) - \delta \omega (d) = d a_i \delta x^i - \delta a_i d x^i = \frac{\partial a_i}{\partial x^k} d x^k \delta x^i - \frac{\partial a_i}{\partial x^k} \delta x^k d x^i .\)
\(\displaystyle d \omega (\delta) - \delta \omega (d) = \sum_{(i, k)} \left( \frac{\partial a_i}{\partial x^k} - \frac{\partial a_k}{\partial x^i} \right) (d x^k \delta x^i - d x^i \delta x^k) .\) (4)

For form is invariant with respect to a change of independent variables, i.e., under a change of the variables \(x^i\) in both parts of (4), the equality will be preserved. Expression (4) is called the bilinear covariant of Frobenius (it is named after the scientist who introduced such forms).

定理 3. If the bilinear covariant of Frobenius of a Pfaffian form \(\omega\) vanishes,

\(\displaystyle d \omega (\delta) - \delta \omega (d) = 0,\) (5)

then the form \(\omega\) is a total differential.

证明. In fact, the vanishing of form (4) for arbitrary differentials \(d x^i\) and \(\delta x^i\) implies that the vanishing of the coefficients on the right-hand side of (4):

\(\displaystyle \frac{\partial a_i}{\partial x^k} - \frac{\partial a_k}{\partial x^i} = 0.\)

It is well known that the last equation is necessary and sufficient for the form

\(\displaystyle \omega = a_i d x^i\)

to be a total differential, \(\omega = d \varphi\).\(\Box\)

定理 4. If the bilinear covariant of a Pfaffian form \(\omega\) vanishes, then the integral

\(\displaystyle u = \int_{M_0}^{M_1} \omega (d)\)

between two points \(M_0 (x_0^1, \ldots, x_0^n)\) and \(M_1 (x_1^1, \ldots, x_1^n)\) does not depend on the choice of a path of integration, i.e., it is path independent. It is assumed that paths of integration connecting two points belong to a continuous family of curves.

证明.

\(\displaystyle x^i = x^i (t, \alpha), \quad i = 1, \ldots, n\)
\(\displaystyle x^i (0, \alpha) = x_0^i \text{ 与 } x^i (1, \alpha) = x_1^i\)

Define two symbols of differentiation:

\(\displaystyle d = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}, \quad \delta = \frac{\partial}{\partial \alpha} .\)
\(\displaystyle \delta d f = \frac{\partial}{\partial \alpha} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} \right) = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial \alpha \partial t}, \quad d \delta f = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial \alpha} \right) = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial t \partial \alpha} .\)

By the theorem hypothesis, the bilinear covariant of Frobenius of the form \(\omega\) vanishes,

\(\displaystyle d \omega (\delta) - \delta d \omega (d) = 0,\)

We set

\(\displaystyle u (t, \alpha) = \int_0^t \omega (d)\) (6)

It follows that

\(\displaystyle \omega (d) = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = d u, \quad \delta \omega (d) = \delta d u,\)

and equaiton (5) takes the form

\(\displaystyle d \omega (\delta) - \delta d u = 0,\)

or by (1), the form

\(\displaystyle d [\omega (\delta) - \delta u] = 0,\)

for any \(t\) and \(\alpha\). Thus

\(\displaystyle \frac{\partial}{\partial t} [\omega (\delta) - \delta u] = 0.\)
\(\displaystyle \omega (\delta) = a_i \frac{\partial x^i}{\partial \alpha} = 0, \quad u = 0,\)

and for each \(t\), we have

\(\displaystyle \omega (\delta) - \delta u = 0.\)

\(\Box\)

Skew-symmetric bilinear forms.

1. The coefficients of skew-symmetric forms are skew-symmetric themselves, i.e., they change their signs under permutation of their indices.

Let

\(\displaystyle F = a_{i j} u^i v^j\) (7)

be a skew-symmetric form. Then after the permutation of variables \(u\) and \(v\), it becomes

\(\displaystyle F = - a_{i j} v^i u^j .\)

Change the index of summation \(i\) for \(j\) and \(j\) for \(i\) in the last equation, we find that

\(\displaystyle F = - a_{j i} v^j u^i .\) (8)

Comparing (7) and (8), we obtain

\(\displaystyle a_{i j} = - a_{j i}, \quad a_{i i} = 0 (i\operatorname{not}\operatorname{summed}) .\)

2. A skew-symmetric form remains skew-symmetric under simultaneous (with the same coefficients) linear substitution of both series of variables.

Consider a substitution

\(\displaystyle U^i = A_k^i u^k, \quad V^i = A_k^i v^k .\)

It is obvious that, under this substitution, the form \(F\) will be transferred into a bilinear form \(\Phi\),

\(\displaystyle F (u, v) = \Phi (U, V) .\)

It is clear that if we change \(u^k\) for \(v^k\), then \(U^i\) will be changed for \(V^i\), and the form \(\Phi\) will change its sign simultaneously with the form \(F\). Hence

\(\displaystyle \Phi (U, V) = - \Phi (V, U) .\)

Exterior quadratic forms.

\(\displaystyle F = \sum_{(i, j)} a_{i j} (u^i v^j - u^j v^i) = \sum_{(i, j)} a_{i j} \left| \begin{array}{cc} u^i & u^j \\ v^i & v^j \end{array} \right| .\)

Introduce a new notation

\(\displaystyle u^i \wedge u^j = \left| \begin{array}{cc} u^i & u^j \\ v^i & v^j \end{array} \right| .\) (9)

We call the product \(u^i \wedge u^j\) the exterior product. The determinant on the right-hand side of (9) is called the value of the exterior product. In order to obtain a value of the exteriro product, it is necessary to give two series of values of the basis elements, \(u^i, u^j\) and \(v^i, v^j\).

All operations on exteriro product are subject to the general rule: any transformation of an expression with exterior product is valid if the values of this expression before and after the transformation are the same for any choice of basis variables.

For example, if we have two linear forms

\(\displaystyle f_1 = a_i u^i, \quad f_2 = b_j u^j,\)

then

\(\displaystyle \begin{array}{rl} f_1 \wedge f_2 & = \left| \begin{array}{cc} f_1 (u) & f_2 (u)\\ f_1 (v) & f_2 (v) \end{array} \right| = \left| \begin{array}{cc} a_i u^i & b_k u^k\\ a_i v^i & b_k v^k \end{array} \right| = (a_i u^i) (b_k v^k) - (a_i v^i) (b_k u^k)\\ & = a_i b_k (u^i v^k - v^i u^k) = a_i b_k \left| \begin{array}{cc} u_i & u^k\\ v^i & v^k \end{array} \right| = a_i b_k u^i \wedge u^k\\ & = \sum_{(i, k)} (a_i b_k - a_k b_i) u^i \wedge u^k . \end{array}\)

推论 5. 1. The exterior product is anticommutative.

2. The exteriro product of equal factors or proportional factors is equal to 0;

\(\displaystyle f \wedge f = 0, \quad f \wedge (c f) = 0.\)

3. A scalar factor can be taken across of an exterior product.

Converse theorems. Cartan's Lemma.

定理 6. The equation

\(\displaystyle f \wedge \varphi = 0,\) (10)

where \(f\) is a given linear form, holds if and only if the form \(\varphi\) is proportional to \(f\):

\(\displaystyle \varphi = c f.\)

定理 7. (Cartan's Lemma) Suppose that \(f^1, \ldots, f^p\) is a given set of linearly independent forms, (i.e., the rank of the matrix of their coefficients is equal to \(p\)). Then the equation

\(\displaystyle f^1 \wedge \varphi_1 + f^2 \wedge \varphi_2 + \cdots + f^p \wedge \varphi_p = 0,\) (11)

holds if and only if the forms \(\varphi_i, i = 1, \ldots, p\) are lienar combinations of the forms \(f^k, k = 1, \ldots, p\), with a symmetric matrix of coefficients:

\(\displaystyle \varphi_i = c_{i k} f^k, \quad c_{i k} = c_{k i} .\) (12)

证明.

\(\displaystyle c_{i k} f^i \wedge f^k + c_{i \lambda} f^i \wedge f^{\lambda} = 0.\) (13)

Skipped.\(\Box\)

定理 8. Suppose that the basis elements \(u^i, i = 1, \ldots, n\) are connected by the equation

\(\displaystyle f = c_i u^i = 0.\) (14)

Then the exterior quodratic form

\(\displaystyle F = \sum_{(i, k)} a_{i j} u^i \wedge u^j\) (15)

vanishes by virtue of (14) if and only if \(F\) has the form

\(\displaystyle F = f \wedge \varphi,\) (16)

where \(\varphi\) is an appropriate chosen linear form.

定理 9. Suppose that the basis elements \(u^i, i = 1, \ldots, n\), are connected by a system of equations,

\(\displaystyle f^i = 0, \ldots, f^p = 0,\) (17)

where \(f^i, i = 1, \ldots, p\), are linearly independent forms. Then the exterior quadratic form \(F\) vanishes by virtue of (17) if and only if

\(\displaystyle F = f^1 \wedge \varphi^1 + \cdots + f^p \wedge \varphi^p .\) (18)

Exterior differential.
Just as a bilinear form can be represented by an exterior quadratic form, a bilinear covariant of Frobenius

\(\displaystyle d \omega (\delta) - \delta \omega (d) = (d a_1 \delta x^1 - \delta a_1 d x^1) + \cdots + (d a_p \delta x^p - \delta a_p d x^p)\)

of the form

\(\displaystyle \omega = a_1 d x^1 + \cdots + a_p d x^p\)

can be represented by the exterior differential

\(\displaystyle d \omega = d a_1 \wedge d x^1 + \cdots + d a_p \wedge d x^p .\) (19)

Let us find the rule of exterior differentiation of the product of a scalar function and a form. Let \(m\) be a function of variables \(x^1, \ldots, x^n\), and \(\omega\) be a linear form

\(\displaystyle \omega = a_i d x^i .\)

Compute the exterior differential of the product \(m \omega\):

\(\displaystyle d (m \omega) = d [(m a_i) d x^i] = d (m a_i) \wedge d x^i .\)

But

\(\displaystyle d (m a_i) = m d a_i + a_i d m.\)

Thus,

\(\displaystyle d (m \omega) = m d (a_i \wedge d x^i) + d m \wedge (a_i d x^i),\)

or

\(\displaystyle d (m \omega) = m d \omega + d m \wedge \omega .\)

entry.title: On the Monge mass transfer problem, Trudinger & Xu-Jia Wang

link.title: On the Monge mass transfer problem, Trudinger & Xu-Jia Wang

entry.content:
On the Monge mass transfer problem

by Neil S. Trudinger, Xu-Jia Wang

Abstract

蒙日在1781年提出的质量传输问题，是从一个质量分布移动到另一个使得一个代价泛函在所有保测度映射中最小。最优传输映射的存在性在1979年由Sudakov用概率论证明。最近Evans和Gangbo发现了一个基于PDE的证明。这篇论文中作者们通过直接从蒙日和康塔洛维奇的势能函数构造一个最优传输的方式给出一个更基本和更短的证明。

1Introduction

给定欧氏空间 ℝn 上的两个有界开集 U,V，和对应的有相同总质量的质量分布，用非负函数f,g∈L1(U),L1(V)分别表示，满足

∫Uf=∫Vg (1.1)

蒙日问题是是否存在一个保测度映射s0:U→V最小化这个代价泛函

𝒞(s)=∫Uf(x)|s(x)-x|d⁡x (1.2)

在所有从U到V的保测度映射中。一个映射s被叫做保测度的如果它是Borel可测的且对任意Borel 集 E⊂V,

∫s-1(E)f=∫Eg, (1.3)

即它相对于测度f⁡d⁡x,g⁡d⁡x是保测度的。在早期蒙日自己的，Apell和Kantorovich的贡献之后，Sudakov在1979年确实地解决了，基于他对概率空间进行分解的研究成果。最近，Evans和Gangbo发现一个用PDE新的证明，在f和g的更强的假设，但和Sudakov一开始的证明一样，它非常复杂。这篇note的目标是提供一个更基本简短的证明。

蒙日问题也已经对其他代价函数考虑过。给定任意连续代价函数c:ℝn×ℝn→ℝ，我们定义一个对应的代价泛函𝒞

𝒞(s)=∫Uf(x)c(x,s(x))d⁡x, (1.4)

其中s∈𝒮=𝒮(f,g)，所有从(U,f⁡d⁡x)到(V,g⁡d⁡y)的保测度映射的集合。一个重要情形是 c(x,y)=c(x-y) 且c是一个严格凸函数，所以 D⁡c 可逆。在特殊的二次代价情形 c(x,y)=|x-y|2，最优映射的正则性也已经证明了。

我们对蒙日传输问题的方法利用了严格凸代价函数的近似，像Caffarelli，Gangbo和McCann那样，传输射线和传输集合的记号来自Evans和Gangbo。这些工作中，方法的关键依赖在于Kantorovich对偶问题的势能函数。我们的想法是构造一个映射 s0，满足质量平衡条件 (1.3)，使得对于传输射线 ℓ 中的每个点 x，s0(x)也落在 ℓ 上。而且，如果 x1,x2∈ℓ,

(s0(x1)-s0(x2))⋅(x1-x2)≥0. (1.5)

我们证明这个映射 s0 确实是最优的。为了构造满足单调性条件(1.5)的映射 s0，我们会使用更简单的严格凸代价函数情形下 cp(x,y)=|x-y|p,p>1的最优传输的存在性。更多蒙日问题的讨论，它有趣的历史和各种应用，读者可以参考[5,7,8,10,14,17]。

2严格凸的情形

要证明质量传输问题在(1.2)的代价函数下的最优传输的存在性，我们需要对应问题在严格凸代价函数下的存在性。我们保持f,g非负Borel可测函数的假设，在 ℝn 上，支撑有界，满足质量平衡条件

∫f=∫g<∞ (2.1)

令𝒮=𝒮(f,g)表示 (ℝn,μ) 到 (ℝn,ν) 的保测度映射集合，μ=f⁡d⁡x,ν=g⁡d⁡x.

Theorem 2.1. Let c∈C1(ℝn) be strictly convex. Then there exists an optimal mapping sc∈𝒮 such that

𝒞(sc)=infs∈𝒮𝒞(s), (2.2)

where

𝒞(s)=∫f(x)c(x-s(x))d⁡x. (2.3)

Furthermore the mapping sc is invertible (almost everywhere), with inverse sc-1∈𝒮(g,f) a minimizer among 𝒮(g,f) of the functional

𝒞'(s)=∫g(x)c(s(x)-x). (2.4)

Proof. 这里简单介绍证明思路，因为后面对c(x)=|x|的证明里还会用到；更多细节见[4,9,17].证明分4步。

  1. 1. 考虑Kantorovich的对偶问题，最大化这个泛函

    I(u,v)=∫u(x)f(x)d⁡x+∫v(y)g(y)d⁡y (2.5)

    在所有下面集合的函数对中

    𝒦={(u,v)∈C(ℝn)|u(x)+v(y)≤c(x-y)⁡∀x,y∈Ω}, (2.6)

    其中Ω 是一个 supp⁡f∪supp⁡g 的有界开邻域。首先我们证明 I(⋅,⋅) 在 𝒦 中有一个最大化函数对 (φ,ψ). 实际上，给定任意 (u,v)∈𝒦，令

    u∗(x) =infy∈Ω{c(x-y)-v(y)}, v∗(y) =infx∈Ω{c(x-y)-u∗(x)}.
